entry.title: How to Integrate UI5 Test Coverage with SonarQube in SAP UI5 Projects
entry.content:
When working with SAP UI5 applications, automated tests are already a good practice. But once the project grows, test execution alone is no longer enough. Teams want to see how much of the application is actually covered by tests, especially in pull requests and CI/CD pipelines.

In this post, I will show how I implemented test coverage reporting for a SAP UI5 application and integrated it with SonarQube so that coverage metrics are visible directly in the quality dashboard.

The solution is based on:

  • ui5-test-runner for running QUnit tests with coverage enabled
  • nyc for generating LCOV reports
  • a custom normalization step to include untested files
  • SonarQube for importing and displaying coverage metrics

This setup works especially well in CI pipelines and helps teams track both coverage on new code and overall project coverage.

Why This Is Needed

In many UI5 projects, unit tests exist, but coverage is not published anywhere centrally. As a result:

  • developers do not see how well new changes are covered
  • pull requests cannot be validated against coverage quality gates
  • SonarQube shows no meaningful UI coverage data
  • uncovered source files may be silently ignored

The main challenge is that in UI5 projects, coverage collection is not always straightforward when tests are executed in the browser. Even if coverage is enabled, the generated report may contain only files that were actually loaded during test execution. This creates an incomplete picture for SonarQube.

To solve this, I implemented an approach that not only generates LCOV coverage but also normalizes it so that all relevant source files are represented, including files with 0% coverage.

Solution Overview

The final flow looks like this:

  1. ui5-test-runner runs QUnit tests with coverage instrumentation
  2. nyc generates the LCOV report
  3. a custom script normalizes the report and adds missing source files
  4. SonarQube imports the LCOV file and displays the coverage metrics

This gives a reliable and CI-friendly setup for SAP UI5 applications.

Prerequisites

Install the required development dependencies:

npm install --save-dev ui5-test-runner nyc puppeteer

What each package is used for

Package Purpose
ui5-test-runnerRuns UI5/QUnit tests and supports code coverage
nycIstanbul-based coverage engine used under the hood
puppeteerRuns tests in headless Chrome, useful for CI

Step 1. Configure ui5-test-runner

Create a ui5-test-runner.json file with the basic setup:

{
 "pageTimeout": 60000,
 "coverage": true,
 "coverageReportDir": "coverage",
 "reportDir": "report",
 "parallel": 2
}

What this configuration does

  • enables coverage collection
  • stores coverage artifacts in the coverage folder
  • defines a timeout for browser-based execution
  • allows parallel test execution for better performance

Step 2. Configure NYC

Even though ui5-test-runner handles much of the work, it relies on NYC internally.
That is why adding an explicit .nycrc.json file is important.

{
 "all": true,
 "sourceMap": false,
 "reporter": ["lcov", "text-summary"],
 "report-dir": "./coverage",
 "temp-dir": "./.nyc_output",
 "include": ["webapp/**/*.js"],
 "exclude": ["webapp/test/**", "webapp/localService/**"],
 "cwd": "."
}

Important points here

  • all: true is important because we want full project visibility
  • test sources are excluded from business code coverage
  • lcov is required because this is the format SonarQube imports

Step 3. Configure SonarQube

Now we need to tell SonarQube where the UI5 sources, tests, and LCOV report are located.

Create or update sonar-project.properties:

 
sonar.projectKey=your-project-key
sonar.projectName=your-project-name

# Source configuration
sonar.sources=webapp/
sonar.exclusions=webapp/localService/**,**/node_modules/**

# Test configuration
sonar.tests=webapp/test/
sonar.test.inclusions=webapp/test/**/*.js

# Coverage report path
sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info
sonar.coverage.exclusions=webapp/test/**,webapp/localService/**

# Encoding
sonar.sourceEncoding=UTF-8

At this point, the most important property is:

sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info

This is the file SonarQube reads to compute the coverage metrics.

Step 4. Add NPM Scripts

The next step is to automate local and CI execution.

In package.json:

{
"scripts": {
"test": "ui5 serve --config ui5-test.yaml --port 8080 & SERVER_PID=$!; sleep 3; ui5-test-runner --url http://localhost:8080/test/unit/unitTests.qunit.html --coverage --coverage-proxy; TEST_EXIT=$?; kill $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true; exit $TEST_EXIT",
"test:ci": "ui5 serve --config ui5-test.yaml --port 8080 & SERVER_PID=$!; sleep 3; ui5-test-runner --url http://localhost:8080/test/unit/unitTests.qunit.html --coverage --coverage-proxy --ci; TEST_EXIT=$?; kill $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true; npm run coverage:normalize; exit $TEST_EXIT",
"coverage:normalize": "node scripts/normalize-coverage.js"
}
}

Why --coverage-proxy is important

When UI5 tests are executed through a served application URL, --coverage-proxy enables on-the-fly instrumentation. Without it, coverage may be missing or incomplete.

Key flags

Flag Meaning
--coverageEnables coverage collection
--coverage-proxyInstruments files dynamically in browser-based execution
--ciUses CI-friendly non-interactive output

Step 5. Normalize Coverage for Untested Files

This was the most important part of the implementation.

The problem

When --coverage-proxy is used, only files that are actually loaded during the test run are instrumented.
That means if a source file is never loaded, it may not appear in lcov.info at all.

For SonarQube, this is problematic because missing files are not always treated the same way as explicitly uncovered files. As a result, the reported coverage can be misleading.

The fix

I added a normalization script that:

  1. scans all source files in webapp
  2. checks which files are already present in lcov.info
  3. adds missing source files with 0% coverage
  4. normalizes paths so that they match SonarQube expectations

Create scripts/normalize-coverage.js:

const fs = require('fs');
const path = require('path');

const lcovPath = 'coverage/lcov.info';
const webappDir = 'webapp';

// Get all JS source files (excluding test and localService)
function getAllSourceFiles() {
const files = [];
function walk(dir) {
const entries = fs.readdirSync(dir, { withFileTypes: true });
for (const entry of entries) {
const fullPath = path.join(dir, entry.name);
if (entry.isDirectory()) {
if (entry.name !== 'test' && entry.name !== 'localService' && entry.name !== 'node_modules') {
walk(fullPath);
}
} else if (entry.name.endsWith('.js')) {
const relativePath = path.relative(webappDir, fullPath).replace(/\\/g, '/');
files.push(relativePath);
}
}
}
walk(webappDir);
return files;
}

// Parse existing lcov.info to get covered files
function parseLcov(content) {
const covered = new Set();
for (const line of content.split('\n')) {
if (line.startsWith('SF:')) {
covered.add(line.slice(3));
}
}
return covered;
}

// Generate lcov entry for uncovered file
function generateUncoveredEntry(filePath) {
const fullPath = path.join(webappDir, filePath);
if (!fs.existsSync(fullPath)) return null;

const content = fs.readFileSync(fullPath, 'utf8');
const lines = content.split('\n');
const executableLines = [];

for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {
const line = lines[i].trim();
if (line &&
!line.startsWith('//') &&
!line.startsWith('/*') &&
!line.startsWith('*') &&
line !== '{' &&
line !== '}' &&
line !== '});' &&
line !== ']);' &&
line !== '};' &&
line !== '"use strict";') {
executableLines.push(i + 1);
}
}

if (executableLines.length === 0) return null;

let entry = `SF:${filePath}\n`;
entry += `FNF:0\nFNH:0\n`;
for (const lineNum of executableLines) {
entry += `DA:${lineNum},0\n`;
}
entry += `LF:${executableLines.length}\nLH:0\n`;
entry += `BRF:0\nBRH:0\n`;
entry += `end_of_record\n`;

return entry;
}

// Main
if (!fs.existsSync(lcovPath)) {
console.log('No lcov.info found, skipping');
process.exit(0);
}

let content = fs.readFileSync(lcovPath, 'utf8');
const lines = content.split('\n');
const normalized = [];

// Normalize paths (remove webapp/ prefix to match sonar.sources)
for (const line of lines) {
if (line.startsWith('SF:')) {
let filePath = line.slice(3);
const webappMatch = filePath.match(/[/\\]?webapp[/\\](.+)$/);
if (webappMatch) {
filePath = webappMatch[1];
}
filePath = filePath.replace(/\\/g, '/').replace(/^\//, '');
normalized.push('SF:' + filePath);
} else {
normalized.push(line);
}
}

// Get already covered files
const coveredFiles = parseLcov(normalized.join('\n'));

// Add uncovered files
const allFiles = getAllSourceFiles();
let uncoveredContent = '';

for (const file of allFiles) {
if (!coveredFiles.has(file)) {
const entry = generateUncoveredEntry(file);
if (entry) uncoveredContent += entry;
}
}

fs.writeFileSync(lcovPath, normalized.join('\n') + '\n' + uncoveredContent);
console.log('Coverage normalized for SonarQube');

A Critical Detail: Path Mapping Between LCOV and SonarQube

This part is easy to overlook, but it is often the real reason why SonarQube shows 0% coverage even when the LCOV file exists.

If SonarQube is configured with:

sonar.sources=webapp/

then SonarQube treats webapp/ as the root of the source tree.

That means:

  • webapp/model/messages.js is indexed by SonarQube as model/messages.js
  • therefore lcov.info must contain:
 
SF:model/messages.js
and not:
SF:webapp/model/messages.js

In other words

The path in SF: must be relative to sonar.sources.

That is exactly why the normalization script rewrites the paths.

CI/CD Integration

Once the local setup works, integrating it into the pipeline is straightforward.

SAP Piper example

In .pipeline/config.yml:

steps:
 npmExecuteScripts:
  install: true
  virtualFrameBuffer: false
  dockerImage: 'cypress/browsers:node-20.18.0-chrome-130.0.6723.69-1-ff-131.0.3-edge-130.0.2849.52-1'
  runScripts:
   - 'test:ci'
  sonarExecuteScan:
  serverUrl: 'https://sonar.tools.sap'

stages:
 Build:
  npmExecuteScripts: true
  sonarExecuteScan: true

GitHub Actions example

- name: Run tests with coverage
run: npm run test:ci

- name: SonarQube Scan
uses: sonarqube-scan-action@v1
with:
args: >
-Dsonar.projectKey=${{ env.PROJECT_KEY }}

Generated Coverage Artifacts

After running:

npm run test:ci

 

you should see:

coverage/
├── lcov.info
├── lcov-report/
│ └── index.html
└── cobertura-coverage.xml

 

What these files are used for

  • lcov.info → imported by SonarQube
  • lcov-report/ → human-readable HTML report for local inspection
  • cobertura-coverage.xml → optional integration with other tools

How Coverage Appears in SonarQube

After the scan, SonarQube usually shows two metrics that are especially important:

Metric Meaning
Coverage on New CodeCoverage only for changed lines in the branch or PR
Overall CoverageCoverage across the entire project

This distinction matters a lot.

A project can have:

  • high coverage on new code, because the changed lines were properly tested
  • low overall coverage, because legacy parts of the application still have poor test coverage

That is not a contradiction. It is normal SonarQube behavior.

Troubleshooting

SonarQube shows 0% coverage

Check the following first:

  1. coverage/lcov.info is generated successfully
  2. sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths points to the correct file
  3. SF: entries match the structure expected by sonar.sources

Useful check:

grep "^SF:" coverage/lcov.info

Some files are missing from coverage

This usually happens because only loaded files were instrumented during the browser run.

That is exactly why the normalization step is needed.

Without normalization, SonarQube may import only a partial coverage picture.

Coverage on new code is high, but overall coverage is low

This is expected and does not necessarily indicate a problem.

It simply means:

  • your recent changes are well tested
  • older application code still lacks coverage

Warning: “Unable to process all coverage”

This may appear when using ui5-test-runner with --coverage-proxy.

In practice, this warning means that not all source files could be discovered automatically during runtime instrumentation.

The custom normalization script compensates for this by adding the missing files manually.

Final Thoughts

For me, the key goal of this implementation was not just to execute UI5 tests with coverage enabled, but to make the results usable and trustworthy inside SonarQube.

The most important lessons from this setup were:

  • generating LCOV is only the first step
  • path alignment between LCOV and sonar.sources is critical
  • browser-based runtime instrumentation does not guarantee full project visibility
  • normalizing missing files makes the SonarQube result much more accurate

With this approach in place, SAP UI5 projects can benefit from the same quality visibility that backend projects usually already have: clear coverage reports, PR metrics, and enforceable quality gates.

References

  • ui5-test-runner Documentation
  • SonarQube JavaScript Coverage Documentation
  • LCOV Format Specification

entry.title: Beginner Guide to SAPUI5: What You Need to Know Before Building Your First App
entry.content:
Hi community, I am a fresher who have been recently onboarded in the SAP UI5 domain. 

It's been a week since I started exploring UI5. In this first week of learning SAPUI5, I focused on understanding the basics that form the foundation of any UI5 application.

In this blog, I want to share what I explored during my first week, what I understood, and how these concepts helped me get more comfortable with SAPUI5 development so that it might help someone who is starting their career in UI5. 

My focus was mainly on understanding the basics — the architecture, environment setup, project structure, simple controls, layouts, and some commonly used complex controls. This week gave me a solid starting point, and I feel much more confident about continuing my learning journey.   Understanding the UI5 Architecture: MVC Pattern

The first concept I explored was the MVC pattern, which is one of the core foundations of SAPUI5 applications.

MVC stands for:

Model View Controller

The View is where the user interface is defined. In SAPUI5, this is often done using XML views.
The Controller handles the logic behind the view — for example, what should happen when a button is clicked.
The Model is responsible for the data that the application works with.

What I found useful about MVC is the way it separates different responsibilities. Instead of keeping everything in one file, SAPUI5 organizes the application in a cleaner and more manageable way. For a beginner, understanding this pattern was very important because it helped me see how different parts of the application connect with each other.

Once I became comfortable with MVC, the overall structure of a UI5 app started looking less confusing.

Setting Up the Development Environment

The next thing I explored was the development environment. 

SAP Business Application Studio SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio is designed with SAP development in mind, so it provides a more guided and integrated experience. It felt like a good option for building SAPUI5 or SAP Fiori applications in a more structured way.

Getting Familiar with the Project Structure

One of the most useful things I learned during this week was the project structure of a SAPUI5 application.

In the beginning, when I saw multiple folders and files, it was a bit difficult to understand which file does what. But once I started learning the purpose of each part, the structure began to feel logical.

Some of the key parts I came across were:

webapp – the main application folder view – where XML views are stored controller – where controller files are written model – used for model-related files manifest.json – the application descriptor Component.js – important for bootstrapping and configuration index.html – the starting point of the application

Understanding the project structure made a big difference for me. It became easier to navigate the application, find files, and understand how the app is launched. For someone new to SAPUI5, I think this is one of the most important areas to understand early, because every other topic becomes easier after that.

Building My First UI5 Application

After learning the basics of architecture and project structure, I moved on to creating my first UI5 application.

This was one of the most interesting parts of my first week because it gave me the chance to apply what I had learned. Even though the application was simple, the process taught me a lot.

I started understanding things like:

how a UI5 app is initialized, how the view connects to the controller, how controls are added to the page, and how user actions are handled.

What I liked most about building the first app was that it turned abstract concepts into something practical. Instead of only reading about views and controllers, I could actually see them working together. That made the learning experience much more meaningful.

Exploring Basic Controls

Once the first app was working, I spent time learning some basic controls in SAPUI5. These included:

Button Input Label Text

These controls may seem simple, but they are the foundation of almost every user interface.

Button

The Button control helped me understand how events are handled in SAPUI5. For example, when a user clicks a button, the action is handled in the controller.

Input

The Input control is used to take user input. It showed me how forms and interactive fields are built in UI5 applications.

Label

The Label control is useful for identifying fields clearly. It plays an important role in making the UI readable and user-friendly.

Text

The Text control is used to display information on the screen. It is simple, but very commonly used in applications.

Working with these basic controls helped me become more comfortable with writing XML views. I also started noticing how each control comes with its own properties and purpose.

Learning Layouts: VBox, HBox, and Grid

After understanding controls, I explored how to organize them properly on the screen using layouts.

A UI may have all the right controls, but without a proper layout, it can still look messy. That is why learning layouts felt like an important step.

VBox

The VBox layout arranges controls vertically, one below another. It is useful when the UI needs a top-to-bottom flow.

HBox

The HBox layout arranges controls horizontally. This is useful when elements need to appear side by side in the same row.

Grid

The Grid layout gives more flexibility in arranging controls and is useful for creating more structured and responsive screens.

This part of my learning helped me understand that UI design is not just about adding controls, but also about placing them in a meaningful and organized way. Layouts are essential for creating clean and usable interfaces.

Exploring Complex Controls: Table, List, and Form

Toward the later part of the week, I started looking at some more advanced or complex controls, including:

Table List Form

These controls gave me a better idea of how SAPUI5 supports real business application requirements.

Table

The Table control is useful when data needs to be displayed in a structured row-and-column format. It is especially important in enterprise applications where users work with large sets of business data.

List

The List control is simpler than a table and works well when items need to be shown in an easy-to-read format.

Form

The Form control is useful for structuring input fields, labels, and other form-related content in a clean way. It helps in building screens where users need to enter or edit information.

Learning these controls helped me see the practical side of SAPUI5 more clearly. Until this point, I was looking at UI5 mostly as a framework with views and controllers. But after exploring tables, lists, and forms, I started seeing how it can be used to build real business-ready applications.

What I Took Away from This First Week

Looking back at my first week with SAPUI5, I feel that I covered some very important foundational topics.

The biggest things I understood were:

the importance of the MVC pattern, the role of a proper development environment, the value of understanding the project structure, how basic controls are used in views, why layouts matter in UI design, and how complex controls support enterprise application needs.

More than anything, this week helped me move from confusion to clarity. I may still be at the beginning of my SAPUI5 journey, but I now feel like I have a base to build on.

Conclusion

My first week of learning SAPUI5 was a really valuable experience. I started with the basics, explored the architecture, set up the environment, understood the project structure, built my first app, and worked with different controls and layouts.

There is still a lot more to learn, but this first step gave me confidence. SAPUI5 seems like a structured and practical framework, especially for building enterprise applications, and I am looking forward to exploring it further.

Thanks for reading. I would be happy to know how your first experience with SAPUI5 was and what topics helped you the most in the beginning. 

I am open for any suggestions and guidance of what are the concepts that I need to focus on for the upcoming week and also any resources that might help me going further.

entry.title: Integrating Google Gemini AI with SAPUI5 Frontend using Node.js Backend
entry.content:
Introduction 

Artificial Intelligence is transforming enterprise applications by enabling intelligent user interactions and automation. In this blog, I will demonstrate how to build an AI-powered chat assistant using: 

  • SAPUI5 for frontend UI  
  • Node.js + Express.js for backend service  
  • Google Gemini API for AI-generated responses  

This application allows users to enter queries in a SAPUI5 chat interface, send them to a backend Node.js service, and receive AI-generated responses dynamically. 

 

Architecture / Flow Explanation 

The application follows a 3-layer architecture 

Frontend (SAPUI5) 

  • Capturing user input 
  • Displaying chat messages 
  • Calling backend REST API 

Backend (Node.js / Express) 

  • Receiving frontend request 
  • Processing user prompt 
  • Calling Gemini API 
  • Sending AI response back 

AI Layer (Google Gemini) 

  • Understanding prompt 
  • Generating intelligent response 

 

Frontend Explanation 

View: 

  • ScrollContainer : For scrolling chat history 
  • VBox :To store chat messages dynamically 
  • Input Field:- User question input 
  • Button: Send message
  • View 
<mvc:View controllerName="frontend.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m">

    <Page id="page" title="GNI Assistant" enableScrolling="false">

        <content>
            <VBox class="chatContainer" height="100%" justifyContent="SpaceBetween">

            <ScrollContainer height="100%" vertical="true">
                <VBox id="chatBox" class="chatMessages" width="100%" />
            </ScrollContainer>
            

        </VBox>
        <HBox class="chatInputBar">
           
                <Input id="inputField"
                    placeholder="Ask anything..."
                    width="800px"
                    submit="onAskAI"/>

                <Button icon="sap-icon://paper-plane"
                    type="Emphasized"
                    press="onAskAI" />
            </HBox>
        </content>

    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

Controller Flow Explanation 

SAPUI5 Controller Logic: 

const sInput = this.byId("inputField").getValue(); -> Retrieves text entered by user. 

  • Displays user message aligned to right. 
           const oUserMsg = new sap.m.HBox({
                justifyContent: "End",
                items: [
                    new sap.m.Text({ text: sInput }).addStyleClass("userMessage")
                ]
            });

 

Shows temporary loading message while backend processes request 

  oChatBox.addItem(oUserMsg);
            this.byId("inputField").setValue("");
            const oThinkingBox = new sap.m.Text({
                text: "Thinking..."
            }).addStyleClass("aiMessage");

            oChatBox.addItem(oThinkingBox);

Sends POST request to backend 

const response = await fetch("http://localhost:3000/mcp/query", {
                    method: "POST",
                    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                    body: JSON.stringify({ input: sInput })
                });
                const data = await response.json();

Controller.js:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], (Controller) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("frontned.controller.View1", {
        /**
         * @override
         * @returns {void|undefined}
         */
        onInit: function () {

        },
        onAskAI: async function () {
            const sInput = this.byId("inputField").getValue();
            const oChatBox = this.byId("chatBox");

            if (!sInput) return;
            const oUserMsg = new sap.m.HBox({
                justifyContent: "End",
                items: [
                    new sap.m.Text({ text: sInput }).addStyleClass("userMessage")
                ]
            });

            oChatBox.addItem(oUserMsg);
            this.byId("inputField").setValue("");
            const oThinkingBox = new sap.m.Text({
                text: "Thinking..."
            }).addStyleClass("aiMessage");

            oChatBox.addItem(oThinkingBox);

            try {
                const response = await fetch("http://localhost:3000/mcp/query", {
                    method: "POST",
                    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                    body: JSON.stringify({ input: sInput })
                });
                const data = await response.json();
                console.log("Full API Data:", data);
                let sRawAiMessage = data.message || data.response || data.data || JSON.stringify(data);

                sRawAiMessage = sRawAiMessage.replace(/</g, "<").replace(/>/g, ">");

                let cleanMessage = sRawAiMessage.replace(/```javascript/g, "").replace(/```/g, "").trim();

                oChatBox.removeItem(oThinkingBox);

                var textV = new sap.m.Text()
                textV.setText(cleanMessage)
                textV.addStyleClass("aiMessage")

                const oAiMsg = new sap.m.HBox({
                    justifyContent: "Start",
                    items: [
                        textV
                    ]
                });

                oChatBox.addItem(oAiMsg);

                setTimeout(() => {
                    const oScroll = this.byId("chatBox").getParent().getDomRef();
                    if (oScroll) {
                        oScroll.scrollTop = oScroll.scrollHeight;
                    }
                }, 100);

            } catch (error) {
                console.error("ERROR:", error);

                oChatBox.removeItem(oThinkingBox);

                oChatBox.addItem(new sap.m.Text({
                    text: " Backend connection failed"
                }).addStyleClass("aiMessage"));
            }
        }
    });
});

Note: The backend URL currently points to localhost for local development.
For production or cloud deployment, update this endpoint with your deployed backend URL

 

Backend Explanation 

AiService.js: 

The aiService.js file is responsible for handling communication between the backend server and the Google Gemini AI API. 
It receives user input, sends it to Gemini, and returns the generated response. 

Step 1: 
Import Gemini Library 

const { GoogleGenerativeAI } = require("@google/generative-ai");

Before using any external package in Node.js, we must import it into our file. 

Here, we are importing the Google Gemini SDK library provided by Google. 

This library contains built-in methods to: 

  • Connect with Gemini AI service  
  • Authenticate using API key  
  • Select AI models  
  • Generate responses from prompts 

GoogleGenerativeAI: 

It is the main class provided by Gemini SDK. 

We use this class to create a Gemini client instance later in the code 

Step 2: 

require("dotenv").config();

This line loads environment variables from the .env file. 

Environment variables are used to store sensitive data like: 

  • API keys  
  • Database passwords  
  • Secret tokens 

Step 3:  

Create Gemini Instance 

const genAI = new GoogleGenerativeAI(process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY);
  • After importing the Gemini SDK, we create a Gemini client instance to establish secure communication with the Google AI service.
  • This is used to Authenticate with Google and Establish connection with Gemini AI 
  • We pass API key here, this tells Google this application is authorized to use Gemini 

Step 4: 

Select Gemini Model 

This specifies which AI model should process the request

  const model = genAI.getGenerativeModel({
            model: "gemini-2.5-flash-lite"
        });

 

Step 5: 

Build Prompt

  • AI cannot generate response unless we send instructions 
  • So that we are giving instructions to the AI 
const prompt = `
                    You are a highly intelligent, adaptive, and user-friendly AI assistant.

                    Your goal is to understand the user's intent and respond in the best possible way.

                    -----------------------------------
                    CORE BEHAVIOR
                    -----------------------------------
                    - Analyze the user's intent before answering
                    - Adapt format dynamically (like ChatGPT)
                    - Be clear, concise, and useful
                    - Do not force a fixed format

                    -----------------------------------
                    RESPONSE STYLE RULES
                    -----------------------------------

                    1. SIMPLE QUESTIONS (e.g., 2+2, capital of India)
                    - Return only the direct answer
                    - No explanation

                    2. PROGRAMMING / CODE
                    - Return only code
                    - No explanation outside code
                    - Keep it clean and minimal
                    - Add comments only if useful

                    SPECIAL CASE (IMPORTANT):
                    - If the request is about arrays, objects, JSON:
                    → Always return as proper code with variable declaration
                    → Example: const data = [...]

                    3. DATA FORMAT CONVERSION (VERY IMPORTANT)
                    - If user asks to convert (JSON ↔ XML ↔ JS object):
                    → Return ONLY the converted format
                    → No explanation
                    → Keep it clean and valid

                    4. FACTS / NEWS / GENERAL INFORMATION
                    - Use headings and bullet points
                    - Keep it structured and readable

                    5. SCIENCE / THEORY / EDUCATION
                    - Explain clearly in simple terms
                    - Use sections if needed
                    - Include formulas if relevant

                    6. MATHEMATICS
                    - If simple → return direct answer
                    - If complex → show step-by-step solution
                    - Keep it clean

                    7. STORY / CREATIVE WRITING
                    - Use paragraph format
                    - Follow word limit strictly
                    - Make it natural and engaging

                    8. USER-SPECIFIC FORMAT
                    - If user gives format, follow it exactly

                    9. ANY OTHER REQUEST
                    - Choose best format automatically
                    - Prioritize clarity and usefulness

                    -----------------------------------
                    STRICT RULES
                    -----------------------------------
                    - Never return code unless it's a coding request
                    - Never return JSON unless explicitly asked
                    - Never mix explanation with code
                    - Do not over-explain simple queries
                    - Always match user intent

                    -----------------------------------

                    User Request: ${input}`;

        const result = await model.generateContent(prompt);
        const response = await result.response;

        return response.text();

Step 6: 

Create .env file: 

Here we need to specify API Key 

GEMINI_API_KEY=AIzaSyBB**********************

To Create API Key: 

  • Click on this link: https://aistudio.google.com/u/1/api-keys?project=gen-lang-client-0621332950 
  • Click on Porjects on left side bar and then click on Create a New Project and provide project name and create a project 

 

  • Then click on API keys and then click on Create API key and select project and create API key  
  • Then copy API key and paste it in .env file 

 

 service.js:

require("dotenv").config();
const express = require("express");
const cors = require("cors");
const mcpRoutes = require("./routes/mcpRoutes");

const app = express();
app.use(cors());
app.use(express.json());

app.use("/mcp", mcpRoutes);

const PORT = 3000;
app.listen(PORT, () => {
    console.log(`GAI Server running on http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});

 

routes/mcpRoutes.js:

const express = require("express");
const router = express.Router();
const { getAIResponse } = require("../services/aiService");
router.post("/query", async (req, res) => {
    try {
        const { input } = req.body;
        const aiReply = await getAIResponse(input);

        res.json({
            success: true,
            message: aiReply
        });
    } catch (err) {
        res.status(500).json({ error: err.message });
    }
});

module.exports = router;

 

service/aiService.js:

const { GoogleGenerativeAI } = require("@google/generative-ai");

require("dotenv").config();

const genAI = new GoogleGenerativeAI(process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY);

async function getAIResponse(input) {
    try {
        const model = genAI.getGenerativeModel({
            model: "gemini-2.5-flash-lite"
        });
        const prompt = `
                    You are a highly intelligent, adaptive, and user-friendly AI assistant.

                    Your goal is to understand the user's intent and respond in the best possible way.

                    -----------------------------------
                    CORE BEHAVIOR
                    -----------------------------------
                    - Analyze the user's intent before answering
                    - Adapt format dynamically (like ChatGPT)
                    - Be clear, concise, and useful
                    - Do not force a fixed format

                    -----------------------------------
                    RESPONSE STYLE RULES
                    -----------------------------------

                    1. SIMPLE QUESTIONS (e.g., 2+2, capital of India)
                    - Return only the direct answer
                    - No explanation

                    2. PROGRAMMING / CODE
                    - Return only code
                    - No explanation outside code
                    - Keep it clean and minimal
                    - Add comments only if useful

                    SPECIAL CASE (IMPORTANT):
                    - If the request is about arrays, objects, JSON:
                    → Always return as proper code with variable declaration
                    → Example: const data = [...]

                    3. DATA FORMAT CONVERSION (VERY IMPORTANT)
                    - If user asks to convert (JSON ↔ XML ↔ JS object):
                    → Return ONLY the converted format
                    → No explanation
                    → Keep it clean and valid

                    4. FACTS / NEWS / GENERAL INFORMATION
                    - Use headings and bullet points
                    - Keep it structured and readable

                    5. SCIENCE / THEORY / EDUCATION
                    - Explain clearly in simple terms
                    - Use sections if needed
                    - Include formulas if relevant

                    6. MATHEMATICS
                    - If simple → return direct answer
                    - If complex → show step-by-step solution
                    - Keep it clean

                    7. STORY / CREATIVE WRITING
                    - Use paragraph format
                    - Follow word limit strictly
                    - Make it natural and engaging

                    8. USER-SPECIFIC FORMAT
                    - If user gives format, follow it exactly

                    9. ANY OTHER REQUEST
                    - Choose best format automatically
                    - Prioritize clarity and usefulness

                    -----------------------------------
                    STRICT RULES
                    -----------------------------------
                    - Never return code unless it's a coding request
                    - Never return JSON unless explicitly asked
                    - Never mix explanation with code
                    - Do not over-explain simple queries
                    - Always match user intent

                    -----------------------------------

                    User Request: ${input}`;

        const result = await model.generateContent(prompt);
        const response = await result.response;

        return response.text();

    } catch (err) {
        console.error("GEMINI ERROR:", err.message);
        return "I hit a snag. Please try again!";
    }
}

module.exports = { getAIResponse };


Output:

 

 

Architecture

FRONTEND (UI5 App)
frontend/
│
├── webapp/
│   ├── controller/
│   │   ├── App.controller.js
│   │   └── View1.controller.js
│   │
│   ├── view/
│   │   ├── App.view.xml
│   │   └── View1.view.xml
│   │
│   ├── model/
│   ├── css/
│   ├── i18n/
│   │
│   ├── Component.js
│   ├── manifest.json
│   └── index.html
│
├── ui5.yaml
├── package.json
BACKEND
backend/
│
├── db/
│   └── schema.cds
│
├── srv/
│   ├── service.cds
│   ├── handlers.js
│   └── server.js
│
├── package.json

Css

.userMessage {
    background: #007aff;
    color: white;
    padding: 8px 12px ;
    border-radius: 10px;
    margin-top: 120px;
    max-width: 1000%;
}
.chatContainer {
    height: 100%;
}


.sapMScrollCont {
    flex: 1;
}

.chatInputBar {
    flex-shrink: 0;
    padding: 30px;
    border-top: 1px solid #ccc;
    background: white;
}

.aiMessage {
    background: #e8e8e7e2;
    padding: 12px;
    border-radius: 10px;
    margin: 5px;
    max-width: 85%; 
    display: inline-block;
    word-break: break-word;
    color: rgba(10, 9, 9, 0.815);
}

.aiMessage pre {
    background-color: #2d2d2d;
    color: #f8f8f2;
    padding: 10px;
    border-radius: 5px;
    overflow-x: auto;
    font-family: "Courier New", Courier, monospace;
    margin: 10px 0;
}

.aiMessage code {
    font-family: "Courier New", Courier, monospace;
    background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.05);
    padding: 2px 4px;
    border-radius: 3px;
}
.btnClass{
    margin-left: 700px;
}

Conclusion

This project demonstrates how SAPUI5 applications can be enhanced with modern AI capabilities by integrating Google Gemini through a Node.js backend service.

By separating the frontend, backend, and AI layers, the application remains scalable, maintainable, and easy to extend for future enterprise use cases.

During development, one key learning was the importance of prompt engineering in controlling AI response formatting and improving user experience.

Possible future enhancements for this application include:

• Maintaining conversation history for contextual chat
• Streaming AI responses in real time
• Deploying backend services to SAP BTP or cloud platforms
• Adding authentication and user-specific personalization

Integrating AI into enterprise applications opens exciting possibilities for intelligent automation, smart assistants, and enhanced productivity within SAP ecosystems.

entry.title: SAP Fiori Development Newsletter issue #40 (May 2026)
entry.content:
The mid-year conference season is upon us with many opportunities for you to meet our development teams and have conversations with your peers about the topics you care about most. Some of our upcoming events include:

  • SAP Sapphire in Orlando on May 11-13
  • SAP UX Innovation Day in Palo Alto on June 11
  • UI5con in St. Leon-Rot on July 14

Join us to learn about using AI to accelerate development, MCP servers, and the latest UX strategy updates from SAP

If you want to join other developers to discuss various traditional and AI-powered Fiori development-related topics from the comfort of your office or home, I encourage you to join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable. Register online or email me for an invitation.

Development News

SAP Fiori elements feature map: what is available in each SAPUI5 version

If you work with SAP Fiori elements, you have probably run into the problem where it is hard to see in which UI5 version a specific feature was introduced. Marian Zeis created a feature map that shows when a feature first appears. It’s updated automatically whenever new versions are released. Explore the feature map.

Key User Adaptation: powerful new features and seamless transition to Developer Extensibility

With key user adaptation, you can change the user interface of SAP Fiori apps directly in the Fiori launchpad. The goal of key user extensibility is to empower business users to be able to adapt their working environment to suit their needs, without having to rely on system administrators. Learn more.

SAP Fiori tools 2603 update: RAP Project Accelerator, walkthrough, adaptation improvements, and more

SAP Fiori tools 2603 delivers improvements across development transparency, onboarding, AI-supported development, and extensibility. Highlights include Project Accelerator support for RAP, a change in navigation from properties to its source, a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code, and the ability to import existing key user changes into an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project. See details.

Introducing UI5 plugins for Claude: AI-powered development for SAPUI5 and OpenUI5

UI5 plugins for Claude enhance UI5 development workflows with AI assistance by embedding current, maintained guidelines directly into the development workflow. The result is that Claude Code provides UI5-accurate suggestions without you having to paste documentation into every conversation. Learn more.

How to migrate UI5 projects to the latest UI5 best practices

This blog post focuses on how UI5 MCP server can support you in migrating your UI5 project easily to the latest best practices with the help of GitHub Copilot Coding Agent. Migrate your project.

Declarative UI Integration Cards - A consistent framework with flexible content

UI Integration Cards are utilized in many SAP solutions. A new blog post shows key qualities of declarative Integration Cards: They offer a range of display options and minimalist interactions, including List Card, Table Card, Timeline, Calendar, Analytical, and Object Card with input elements. Read more.

More power, new name: The UI Flexibility Service explained

SAPUI5 flexibility allows developers, key users, and end users to adapt standard apps with simple drag-and-drop tools. Since it’s not only for key users, we renamed this capability to UI flexibility service instead of the older, potentially confusing name UI5 flexibility for key users. Learn more.

Navigating from an SAP Fiori elements application to another application

While the SAPUI5 documentation explains how to navigate to other applications in detail, the code snippets sometimes make it difficult to visualize how each navigation pattern behaves in the UI. Mio Yasutake created a sample repository that demonstrates various Fiori elements navigation patterns along with their actual UI behavior. See how.

Events

SAP UX Innovation Days are back for 2026

SAP UX Innovation Day Silicon Valley – Thursday, June 11

Join us at SAP UX Innovation Day Silicon Valley to learn about the intersection between AI and user experience. Our goal is to empower you with knowledge on how to build SAP Fiori apps for SAP S/4HANA Cloud and all the new AI functionality that we are building into the SAP Fiori elements framework. We will cover AI for business users and AI-powered tools for application developers.

Register and more details.

Mark your calendar for other SAP UX Innovation Days in your region:

Dublin, Ireland - Wednesday, September 30

Walldorf, Germany - Thursday, December 10

Other Events

Sapphire – lots of great UX sessions

UI5con - A vibrant gathering for UI5 enthusiasts to connect, learn, and innovate

Back issues from the past year

SAP Fiori Development newsletter March 2026 (issue #39)

SAP Fiori development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38)

SAP Fiori development newsletter November 2025 (issue #37)

SAP Fiori development newsletter September 2025 (issue #36)

SAP Fiori development newsletter July 2025 (issue #35)

SAP FIori development newsletter May 2025 (Issue #34)

entry.title: Cross App Navigation: Friendly Navigation in FLP - Quick Guide
entry.content:
Introduction:

This blog is going to help the people who just started the journey in SAPUI5/Fiori. It’s very basic level content which would help the beginners to understand Cross App Navigation and how it works.

In enterprise SAP applications, navigation isn’t just about moving between pages. it’s about maintaining consistency, security and scalability across multiple apps. That’s where the FLP friendly approach came into the picture.

If you are building apps with SAPUI5 inside the SAP Fiori Launchpad, relying on raw URLs or window.location is a shortcut that will eventually cause problems. Instead, SAP provides a structured service for cross-application navigation.

 

Overview:

Cross-App Navigation is a way for one app to open another app by describing what you want to do, not where it is. It also known as Intent-Based Navigation

One Liner: Cross-App Navigation = Navigate by intention, not by URL, with FLP resolving the correct app dynamically. 

As a quick analogy which will help you to understand easily and recall it wisely.

Imagine that you are going to a hospital for the first time, you wouldn’t usually say/ask that you have to go to building B, floor 3, room 27. Instead you would prefer to say,  I need to see a cardiologist. Then the hospital directs you correctly.

That’s exactly how navigation works in SAP using semantic intent. We have to describe what we have to do, not where it is.

                                                                                       Picture 1: SAP Fiori Navigation

 

Key Concepts

1. Semantic Object - Represents a business entity (e.g., Supplier, SalesOrder)

2. Action - Defines what you want to do (e.g., display, create, manage)

3. Parameters - Used to pass data between apps (like SupplierId)

 

How it works?

Let’s understand how it works. SAP recommends using the CrossApplicationNavigation service, which actually  works on semantic objects and actions.

                                                                                  Picture 2: SAPUI5 Intent based Navigation

 

Example Snippet:

Source App:
// In our Source or current app:
sap.ushell.Container.getService("CrossApplicationNavigation")
  .toExternal({
    target: {
      semanticObject: "SupplierQuotation",
      action: "display" // action can be like anything (display or manage)
    },
    params: {
      supplierId: "SUP123" // As usual Key value pair, Here SalesOrderId is Key Property & 12345 is the value
      isActive: false // Please convert this as Boolean value at target app
    }
  });

                                                                                 Snippet 1: Source App Code - To do Cross App

Target App:
// In our Target App: 
onInit: function () {
const oComponentData = this.getOwnerComponent().getComponentData();
const oParams = oComponentData?.startupParameters;
if (oParams && oParams.supplierId) {
const sSupId = oParams.supplierId?.[0]; // always array
const bIsActive = Boolean(isActive?.[0]);  // always array & we are converting to boolean using Boolean(X)
console.log("Supplier Id:", sSupId);
console.log("Is Active:", bIsActive ? “Yes” : “No”);
}
}

                                                                             Snippet 2: Target App Code - To handle parameters

This is how we can retrieve it and handle the data.

 

What Happens Behind the Scenes?

When you trigger navigation:

  • FLP resolves the intent (#SupplierQuotation)
  • Matches it to a configured app
  • Launches the correct application
  • Passes parameters securely 

 

URL Understanding: (Let’s Breakdown)

#SupplierQuotation-display?supplierId=SUP123&isActive=true

                                                                  Picture 3: SAPUI5 Intent based Navigation - URL Understanding

For your understanding:
URL      => #SupplierQuotation-display?supplierId=SUP123&isActive=true 
#        => means FLP Routing trigger
Supplier => means Semantic Object
display  => means Action
?        => means start of the parameters
supplierId=SUP123&isActive=true  => means Business Data

/*IMPORTANT*/
How it basically passes: {
supplierId: ["SUP123"],
isActive: ["true"]
}

                                                                          Snippet 3: URL Understanding Notes

Why Not Use Direct URLs?

We can navigate like this: window.location.href = "/sap/bc/ui5_ui5/sap/Z_SALES_APP/index.html?orderId=123";

But this approach:

  • Breaks in FLP environments
  • Ignores role-based navigation
  • Doesn’t integrate with intent-based routing
  • Becomes hard to maintain across landscapes

 

Benefits of FLP Navigation:

  •  Role-based access control
  •  Loose coupling between apps
  •  Centralized navigation configuration
  •  Works across systems (Dev, QA, Prod)
  •  Supports deep linking & bookmarks

 

Challenges of FLP Navigation:

While handling SAPUI5 navigation parameter, we have to keep in our mind, that values are always  wrapped as arrays, String representation even though  it got passed as Number or Boolean and so we have to convert it properly in the target app.

                                                                 Picture 4: SAPUI5 Navigation Parameter Handling Challenges

 

When Should You Use This?

Use FLP navigation when:

  • Moving between SAP Fiori apps
  • Working inside Launchpad
  • Building enterprise-grade solutions

Avoid it only if you’re building a standalone UI5 app outside FLP

 

Conclusion:

Overall, cross app navigation in FLP separates intent from implementation, allowing applications to remain loosely coupled, easier to maintain and aligned with enterprise-level standards.

Hope that you got some idea about Cross app navigation in SAPUI5 in FLP Intent way.

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog! If you found this helpful, i would love to hear your thoughts, feedback or questions in the comments. Let's keep learning and growing together!

I’m still new to blogging, so if you notice anything that could be improved or corrected, please don’t hesitate to let me know!!

entry.title: Recovering UI5 adaptation project source from a deployed system
entry.content:
Introduction

UI5 adaptations can keep running in SAP long after the original developer and their local project are gone. This post explains why you cannot treat adaptations like ordinary BSP content, how adaptation files are actually stored, and how we automated downloading them from the layered repository into a local folder. You will learn which database table holds the data, what our small ABAP report does, and what you still need to set up by hand before using the files in your IDE.

The situation

Imagine you are asked to implement additional functionality for an already extended standard UI5 application. The extension is running in the system and users rely on it. The problem is that the team never used proper source code management, and the developer who built the adaptation has left the company—along with the local project files.

So the adaptation exists in SAP and the deployment is there. In theory you only need to “download it” again. In practice this is a bit awkward: there is no single, obvious option comparable to something like /UI5/UI5_REPOSITORY_LOAD that pulls the whole project. A main reason is that these adaptation files are not stored in the BSP repository; they live in the layered repository.

Inspecting files manually

You can inspect adaptation files one by one using the Layered Repository option in transaction SUI_SUPPORT (thanks to @abdullahgunes for pointing this out). 

You can view individual files, download them manually, and rebuild the folder structure yourself. That works, but it becomes tedious as soon as the adaptation has more than a handful of files.

An automated approach

We looked for a more automated way and ended up with a small ABAP report that reads UI5 adaptation files directly from the layered repository and writes them to a local folder on your machine.

The important technical detail: files in the layered repository are persisted in database table /UIF/LREPDCONT. That table holds what you need to reconstruct a project on disk: the namespace (including the project identifier and folder structure), each file’s name and type, and the content in binary form.

The report: ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD

We created report ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD and kept it deliberately simple. It has a single main input: the custom app (variant) ID. You can type it or use value help. When you run the report and choose a target folder, the program downloads all related files and recreates the original folder structure on your local machine.

Limitation: dist folder only

You should be aware of one limitation: at present, only the contents of the dist (which is created as a result of the build step) folder are stored in the layered repository in a way this approach recovers. This is enough to recreate the project as these are same with the contents of the webapp folder. You still need to create a dummy UI5 adaptation project in your IDE and then move the downloaded  content to the webapp folder into that project so you do not miss files such as ui5.yaml & package.json.

Reference implementation

The full source for the report is available here:

https://github.com/onurakca/SAPUI5-Adaptation-Project-Downloader

The report is provided free of charge under the MIT License. Use it at your own risk: test in non-production systems first, verify behavior in your landscape, and ensure compliance with your organization’s rules for custom ABAP and repository access.

Conclusion

This post walked through how UI5 adaptations are tied to the layered repository rather than classic BSP download flows, how table /UIF/LREPDCONT stores the files you need, and how ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD bulk-exports them while preserving project structure. You now know the gap between “deployment exists in SAP” and “IDE-ready project,” including the webapp-only limitation and the step of dropping files into a new adaptation shell. Even with that limitation, this can save a lot of time when you inherit an adaptation with no Git history and need to review, compare, or extend it quickly.

entry.title: UI5 MCP Servers Setup Guide + Real Developer Experience
entry.content:
Introduction 

As a developer working on a hybrid UI5 project, I explored and configured UI5 MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers to improve my workflow. What started as a setup exercise quickly turned into a major productivity upgrade. 

This guide combines a step-by-step setup tutorial with real-world experience, so you not only know how to set it up, but also why it actually matters. 

What You’ll Get 

After setting this up, you’ll have: 

  • Instant access to UI5 API documentation  
  • Component discovery without opening a browser  
  • On-demand code examples  
  • Built-in best practices guidance  
  • TypeScript-aware development support  
  • Everything directly inside your IDE  

 

My Project Context 

I worked on a hybrid application: 

  • Traditional UI5 App (SAPUI5)  
  • React App using UI5 Web Components  

This setup made it clear how both MCP servers complement each other. 

Prerequisites 

Before starting, ensure you have: 

  • Visual Studio Code (v1.85 or later)  
  • Node.js (v18 or later)  

Verify installation: 

node --version   
npm --version   

Step 1: Install Cline Extension 

Cline acts as the AI assistant that connects to MCP servers. 

Option A: From VS Code 

  • Open VS Code  
  • Go to Extensions (Ctrl+Shift+X)  
  • Search for Cline  
  • Install the extension by Saoud Rizwan  
  • Restart VS Code if required  

Option B: Command Line 

code --install-extension saoudrizwan.claude-dev 

Verify 

  • Open sidebar  
  • Click Cline icon  
  • You should see a chat interface  

Step 2: Configure MCP Servers 

Open settings.json and add: 

"@ui5/mcp-server": { 

      "autoApprove": [ 

        "get_guidelines", 

        "run_ui5_linter" 

      ], 

      "disabled": false, 

      "timeout": 60, 

      "type": "stdio", 

      "command": "npx", 

      "args": [ 

        "-y", 

        "@ui5/mcp-server" 

      ] 

    }, 

    "ui5-wcr": { 

      "autoApprove": [ 

        "create_app", 

        "get_component_api", 

        "get_documentation", 

        "get_public_utils", 

        "list_components" 

      ], 

      "timeout": 60, 

      "type": "stdio", 

      "command": "npx", 

      "args": [ 

        "@ui5/webcomponents-react-mcp@latest" 

      ] 

    }

 

Save the file. 

Step 3: Restart VS Code 

This step is mandatory. 

  • Close VS Code  
  • Reopen  
  • Open your project  

 

Step 4: Verify Installation 

Try these in Cline: 

  • “List available MCP tools”  
  • “Get UI5 guidelines”  
  • “List UI5 components”  
  • “What are props for Dialog?”  

If everything works, you’re ready. 

My Experience Using MCP Servers 

1. Instant API Access 

Before: constant browser switching 
Now: everything inside IDE 

Example: 
Query any UI5 component and instantly get props, events, and methods. 

2.  Component Discovery 

Finding the right component used to be slow. 

Now I can: 

  • Filter by category  
  • Explore 200+ components  
  • Get import statements instantly  

 3. Built-in Linting 

The UI5 linter helps: 

  • Detect deprecated APIs  
  • Suggest fixes  
  • Maintain clean code  

This was especially useful in traditional UI5 code. 

4. Best Practices on Demand 

Using guidelines: 

  • Avoid common mistakes  
  • Follow proper architecture  
  • Build scalable UI5 apps  

 5. Rapid App Creation 

With MCP tools, I could: 

  • Generate apps with templates  
  • Auto-configure TypeScript  
  • Setup projects instantly  

 

 

Real Workflow Impact 

Before 

  • Write code  
  • Open browser  
  • Search docs  
  • Copy info  
  • Return to IDE  
  • Fix issues manually  

After 

  • Write code  
  • Ask AI  
  • Get instant answers  
  • Auto-fix issues  
  • Continue in flow  

Time saved: ~30–40% 

 

Key Benefits I Noticed 

  • No context switching  
  • Faster debugging  
  • Cleaner code  
  • Better architecture decisions  
  • Offline documentation access  

 

 

Challenges 

  • Initial setup takes effort  
  • Learning tool usage takes time  
  • Understanding when to use which tool  

But once past that, the experience is smooth. 

 

Best Practices 

Do 

  • Use guidelines before building features  
  • Run linter regularly  
  • Use component discovery tools  
  • Query APIs instead of Googling  

Don’t 

  • Ignore linter warnings  
  • Skip best practices  
  • Use wrong tools for the task  

 

Why npx is Used 

  • No global install needed  
  • Always runs latest version  
  • Quick execution  

 

Troubleshooting 

Common issues and fixes: 

  • MCP not working → Restart VS Code  
  • npx not found → Install Node.js  
  • Slow response → First-time download delay  
  • No panel → Open Cline manually  

 

Final Thoughts 

MCP servers fundamentally changed how I work with UI5. 

They: 

  • Reduced friction  
  • Improved code quality  
  • Made development faster and smarter  

For any developer working with UI5 or React-based UI5 components, this setup is absolutely worth it. 

References & Useful Links

  • UI5 Official Documentation
    https://ui5.sap.com/
  • UI5 SDK (API Reference)
    https://ui5.sap.com/#/api
  • UI5 Web Components for React
    https://sap.github.io/ui5-webcomponents-react/
  • UI5 MCP Server (npm)
    https://www.npmjs.com/package/@ui5/mcp-server
  • UI5 Web Components MCP Server
    https://www.npmjs.com/package/@ui5/webcomponents-react-mcp
  • Visual Studio Code
    https://code.visualstudio.com/
  • Node.js
    https://nodejs.org/
  • Cline Extension (VS Code Marketplace)
    https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=saoudrizwan.claude-dev
  • SAP Fiori Design Guidelines
    https://experience.sap.com/fiori-design/
  • OpenUI5 GitHub Repository
    https://github.com/SAP/openui5

entry.title: Upgrading your SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor: Beyond TinyMCE version 4
entry.content:
Rich Text Editor in SAPUI5

The Rich Text Editor is an SAPUI5 control for data input and editing. It is used to enter rich text with different styles, colors, and formatting. The Rich Text Editor allows users to format text and add different types of elements, such as images and hyperlinks.

Screenshot of the SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor control

See details on

  • sap.ui.richtexteditor.RichTextEditor: Design, documentation, API reference and samples
  • RichTextEditor building block: Documentation and documentation in SAP Fiori Development Portal

Dependency on TinyMCE

For its implementation, this control uses the third-party component TinyMCE (see https://www.tiny.cloud/). When Tiny deprecates a version of TinyMCE, this version might still be in use in some versions of SAPUI5. Should issues arise, the SAPUI5 team tries to fix them by working around gaps. However, sometimes this is not possible, and updating TinyMCE is necessary, even in maintenance versions of SAPUI5. This is especially important for security, an area where SAPUI5 must address upcoming risks for all customers.

This dependency adds restrictions to the usage and support of the Rich Text Editor control, see the documentation. If your app uses a deprecated version of TinyMCE, the console shows a respective error message stating that this version of TinyMCE is deprecated and will be removed in future.

What happens in July 2026?

 

SAPUI5 will stop loading the deprecated TinyMCE version 4 by default.
Instead, it will load the latest available version.

This will happen in the following SAPUI5 versions:

  • SAPUI5 1.71   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.71.62
  • SAPUI5 1.84   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.84.42
  • SAPUI5 1.96   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.96.28
  • SAPUI5 1.108 - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.108.0 

What does this mean for you?

As always, you benefit from improved features and qualities of the SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor. If, and only if, your app relies on TinyMCE version 4, the app will break until you use the EditorType property to explicitly stay with version 4. SAP does not recommend using deprecated software, as explained above.

What is planned for 2026?

The SAPUI5 team further plans to permanently remove TinyMCE version 4 from the SAPUI5 code later in 2026. This means there will be no way to load it anymore, no will workaround available. SAP strongly recommends removing any dependency to TinyMCE version 4.

Call to Action

Avoid any dependency on a specific (deprecated) version of TinyMCE to always benefit from the latest features and qualities in your SAPUI5 apps.

entry.title: A conversation with Takvor Tilkiyan, an SAPUI5 Application Development Enthusiast
entry.content:
This blog post is an informal conversation between a student and a teacher. The student wants to understand what has sparked his teacher's drive to learn and use the SAPUI5 framework as a hobby.  In a technology landscape dominated by AI's efficiency, human stories remain one of the great tools to ignite passion for learning, creativity, and optimism. Our hope is that this conversation will help readers learn more about the SAPUI5 framework while getting to know Takvor and his journey as an enthusiast of the SAPUI5 community.

Hello Takvor, in 2025, I attended your "SAPUI5 Application Development" one-day training session and was delighted to be part of it. I understand that you organize and deliver these trainings based on your experience with SAPUI5 and a genuine passion for this technology. Could you share your background and how you got started with SAPUI5 as a hobby? What led you to begin using it?

Takvor: I initially started using SAPUI5 as part of my tasks on one of our SAP projects. However, this was in the early days of SAPUI5 when there were no mobile controls yet. After about a year and a half of working with the framework, I applied for a one-month fellowship within SAP. During this fellowship, I helped SAPUI5 colleagues develop applications using the freshly released SAP m mobile controls.

This time required me to deepen my knowledge of SAPUI5, which truly ignited my enthusiasm for the framework. I realized that using a unified framework like SAPUI5 allows the development of applications accessible from various devices and hosted on a web server. This means that SAPUI5 applications are easy to maintain and accessible to all users and devices. This discovery ignited my interest in further developing my knowledge and diving deeper into the framework.

I read all available SAPUI5 documentation, developer guides, and related topics. I was eager to put this knowledge into practice, and the following year, there was a competition to develop an application that had to be rendered on a mobile device. So, I decided to develop the UI for such an application using SAPUI5 m controls to practice the knowledge I had gained. Initially, I focused on developing the user interface and participating in the competition.

The application itself was interesting; it was about sharing information on who drives what car in the company (Carfleet). So, if you wanted to buy a car, you could find someone who owned that model and seek advice.

Next, I further developed this application by adding a back end. Eventually, this application became generally available to all colleagues within SAP. At the same time, the UI was developed in accordance with all SAPUI5 documentation standards, so I used it as a basis for the training I started. I wanted to share the SAPUI5 knowledge I had gained with other colleagues, so I launched the training program.

That's how it all came together— I developed the training using my experience developing this application, meaning the application serves as a practical example of how best practices are applied. That's my background related to SAPUI5.

 

What challenges did you face while learning SAPUI5 as a framework, and how did you overcome them? I find this question particularly interesting because, as I understand it, you've been learning by doing—essentially self-taught and figuring things out on your own.

So, when considering overcoming these challenges, I believe it's important for young learners to understand not just the external obstacles, but also to understand their own internal motivation. Apart from the challenges, what led to your success? Was it curiosity, persistence, or other personal traits? If you could name some qualities that helped you succeed, that would be great!

Takvor: Yeah, sure! My main job at SAP isn't related to UI technologies at all. Throughout the projects I've participated in, I've had some UI-related tasks after the initial project, but maybe 80% or 90% of my work is back-end. Right now, I'm working with Golang on the Signify service. This is a Hyperspace service used to digitally sign and verify artifacts at SAP. We don't have a graphical user interface—it's all API-based.

I'm saying this to highlight that my experience with SAPUI5 is entirely hobby-based. It's serious, yet one of my passions and interests that I want to keep alive, even as new developments emerge.

Regarding time spans, I didn't have deadlines for developing my SAPUI5 applications or expanding the training course content. I was doing it at my own pace, reading all new topics in the SAPUI5 documentation. That's probably one reason that helped me learn—there was no pressure, which usually isn't the case. However, if someone wants to learn SAPUI5, there's plenty to learn and various ways to do so.

The documentation—the Demo Kit—is filled with guidelines and examples. Another great aspect of UI technology is that you get immediate, interactive examples you can inspect and directly interact with. There are samples within the Demo Kit where you can modify the code—or copy, paste, change, and start using it immediately.

There is a wealth of information about SAPUI5, and with time and interest, you can explore and understand it. The topics are complex because the framework is comprehensive, covering everything needed for enterprise applications. The toolset and utilities are all there. The good thing about the framework is that it's very well structured. While it might be complex, the topics are logically connected and make sense. Once you learn the fundamentals, you'll be able to build your tasks or UIs as you wish.

Compared to other frameworks, can you give an example for those interested in learning SAPUI5? How can we illustrate this complexity, perhaps in relation to another technology? Is there an example we can provide to help them understand?

Takvor: Sure. One example is the sheer number of formats and languages you need to know while building an SAPUI5 application. You're primarily working with JavaScript, but you also need to understand XML for views. You should know HTML here and there—not extensively, but somewhat. CSS is also necessary. YAML is another important format, mainly for setting up your development environment, builds, and local servers. JSON is used in many areas—not directly everywhere, but still important to be familiar with. These formats contribute to the complexity.

Another aspect is the baseline artifacts required to build even a simple but standard application. You can create a basic application with just one HTML file—that's fine—but in practice, that's not how most applications are built. Most are structured as components with component descriptors, views, a root view, etc.

For example, if you want to create a standard application with simple functionality like displaying "Hello World"—just to show something on the UI—you first create the index.html. You'll then need a Component.js, which describes your component because applications are either embedded in LaunchPads or run as independent units. Next, you'll need a manifest.json to configure this component and set up routes to the application. In the manifest.json, you specify your root view, which is like a container for all other views. Then, in this root view, you start laying out different pages represented by views and controllers.

That structure alone is complex, and the multitude of layers may sound intimidating. On each level, different formats and configurations exist. For example, in manifest.json, you should learn how routes are configured. In the root view, you should know your root container. In Component.js, it's often straightforward—mostly copy-pasting—but if you initialize some models in Component.js, you place them there. Index.html might be the simplest of all these levels, but even then, you need a well-configured script to bootstrap the application.

All this contributes to the complexity, which might confuse many colleagues. Yes, it's rewarding when you connect all these details and observe the results, such as applications for the car fleet or skills transfer across the organization. A major advantage of SAPUI5 is its foundation on open standards like JavaScript, HTML, and CSS. Most of these technologies are open standards, making the framework technology-agnostic. Even if you require something specific—like an external library or UI controls—you can easily integrate these libraries into the standard framework as they're built on open standards.

You mentioned three levels of reusability. Could you describe the three levels of reusability and the standardization you mentioned in SAPUI5?

Takvor: Yeah, there are three approaches to consuming SAPUI5. At SAP, we aim for all applications to have a consistent look and feel. This is the basic goal—the minimum requirement is that your application uses standard themes and has a uniform appearance.

First Level of Reusability: This is Fiori Elements. If you want just this, you can use Fiori Elements. I think they're based on web components. I've not used them in my apps—I'm using the SAP controls directly provided by the framework. But if your team consists of developers familiar with Angular or React and insists on using them, you can still build your UI in those frameworks while achieving the standard SAP look and feel. This is the first way to consume SAPUI5, the first level of reusability.

Second Level of Reusability: This is the full SAPUI5 Framework. On the other hand, the SAPUI5 framework is a comprehensive UI framework. Using only it, you can build a full-fledged enterprise application featuring a standard look, controls standard to UI5 developers, Model-View-Controller architecture, JSON models, other data models, and internationalization—essentially all major features needed in a UI application are provided. I employ this approach in my two applications. It's intended for standard UI developers who want all features SAPUI5 provides.

Third Level of Reusability: These are the Smart Controls. The third type of reusability involves Smart Controls. This approach is for developers with little or no UI experience. For example, ABAP developers who have decades of back-end experience but have never worked on UI development can use a limited set of SAPUI5 controls called Dynamic Controls, configuring appearance, behavior, and interaction through metadata. They build their backend or data service as preferred, using instructions or annotations interpreted by Smart Controls—UI controls that deliver intended functionality. This approach is often used when building something standardized, with no deviations in look, feel, and interaction, relying mostly on back-end logic.

To summarize:

  • The first approach targets UI developers experienced in popular frameworks but less familiar with the SAPUI5 complexity.
  • The second level is intended for standard UI developers seeking the full power of SAPUI5.
  • The third level is designed for back-end developers requiring minimal front-end development for UI creation.

Are there any controls or parts of the technology that are favorites of yours? Do you have controls that you particularly enjoy using? Are there controls you believe could be improved? What are your thoughts?

Takvor: Yeah, one of the most useful controls is the FlexBox controls in SAPUI5, based on the popular CSS Flexible Box Layout standard. They include HBox (horizontal box) and VBox (vertical box), serving as highly configurable and responsive areas or containers. When you shrink your browser or open the app on a mobile device, the controls inside automatically rearrange. With FlexBox, you can easily build your layout.

For example, if I need cards or forms—like when dealing with a dialog containing a form—I use FlexBox as it's simple and flexible.

Regarding not just controls, but also the eventing system—the Event Bus, specifically—is very useful. I aim to keep the different views and components of the app independent to avoid hard references or API ties. This is achieved when having a dialog with a view and controller, and a view showing this dialog. To keep the dialog and logic separate from the parent view, their connection is easily managed by the Event Bus.

For example, if you wish to pass data or trigger an event from the dialog to the view and then process it, you simply send it as an Event Bus event. This separates UI components, making the application cleaner and more organized.

Since you conduct SAPUI5 training sessions, what is your impression of how participants respond to the technology? Is there something you notice people particularly like, or something that challenges them? 

Takvor: I think the most challenging part for colleagues in my training courses is SAPUI5 bindings. We spend significant time explaining binding methods—the various syntax options for achieving the same or complex bindings. This topic engages them greatly, and it's where they often make mistakes.

I've observed that colleagues feel happy when they understand connections. Once they grasp what ties together and how things connect, they gain control over the technology. As mentioned earlier, the framework is complex but logical and well-structured. With enough time and attention, one can build a mental model of the application. Upon mastering this model and gaining experience—for example, knowing available controls, tools, and events—you start working with confidence.

In our preparation for this conversation, we discussed the UI5 community and support network, noting pros and cons. How does the SAPUI5 framework compete with other, likely larger communities? As an enterprise framework, what advantages does SAPUI5 and its community offer?

Takvor: If you aim to build something within the SAP environment, SAPUI5 is a great choice as it's the standard framework. Although it's more complex than React or Angular, most SAP applications are built with it. Internally at SAP, examples and support abound.

The framework is not only well-structured but also based on open standards. Suppose you encounter difficulties with an aspect—FlexBox, for instance—if documentation lacks information, you can explore open standards, like standard CSS Flexible Box controls, for online support.

Nearly all SAPUI5 controls are based on widely-used open standards, providing opportunities to understand and extend them as needed. The level of standardization in SAPUI5 technologies is very high.

How often do you offer the SAPUI5 Application Development training, and how can people join if interested?

Takvor: We plan to offer the "SAPUI5 Application Development" training twice a year, likely mid-year and in the second half. Colleagues can follow the newsletter in the section "Trainings in Focus." 

The approach is practical and hands-on—though not wholly hands-on—but focuses on diving into real application code and learning concepts. That's the training style.

entry.title: How to Restrict CRUD Operations in SAP CAPM Using @Capabilities, @readonly and Test APIs with .http
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, 

Today, I would like to share a blog about restricting CRUD operations in SAP CAPM using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotations and testing APIs with .http files.
