In SAP CAPM, entities are automatically exposed as OData services with CRUD operations. However, in many business scenarios, developers need to control which operations users are allowed to perform on specific entities. 

Using '@Capabilities', we can control Insert, Update, and Delete operations individually for entities. Using '@readonly', we can make an entity completely read-only by allowing only GET operations. These annotations help protect business data from unwanted modifications and improve API security. 

Below, I’ll guide you through a simple step-by-step process to implement CRUD restrictions using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly', and test APIs directly inside BAS using .http files. 

Overview 

CRUD stands for: 

Create → POST  
Read → GET  
Update → PATCH / PUT  
Delete → DELETE  
By default, SAP CAPM allows all CRUD operations for entities exposed through services. 

In real-world applications, this can create issues such as: 

Users accidentally deleting records  
Unauthorized updates to important data  
Business rules requiring read-only entities  
To solve this, SAP CAPM provides the '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotation. 

Using this annotation, developers can: 

Allow or restrict insert operations  
Allow or restrict update operations  
Allow or restrict delete operations  
Make entities completely read-only using '@readonly'  
Protect business 
For demonstration purposes, I am using an employee entity, for example. 

Step 1: Create Employee Entity 


Open the following file: 

db/datamodel.cds 

Add the following Employee entity: 

namespace dip.db; 
using { cuid , Currency} from '@sap/cds/common'; 
using { dip.common } from './common'; 

type Guid : String(32); 

context master { 
entity employee : cuid
{ 
    nameFirst: String(40); 
    nameMiddle: String(40); 
    nameLast: String(40); 
    nameInitials: String(40); 
    sex : common.Gender; 
    language: String(5); 
    phoneNumber: common.PhoneNumber; 
    email: common.Email; 
    loginName: String(20); 
    Currency: Currency; 
    salaryAmount: common.AmountT; 
    accountNumber: String(16); 
    bankId: String(8); 
    bankName: String(64); 
    validFrom: Date; 
    validTo: Date; 
} 
}
 
 Step 2: Expose Entity in Service Layer 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Add the following code: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 
service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService')  

{    
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee;
}
 
Step 3: Create Common Types File 

Before exposing the entity in the service layer, we will create a reusable common file for shared data types and validations. 

In SAP CAPM, common.cds is used to define reusable: 

  • custom types  
  • validations  
  • enums  
  • aspects  

This helps reduce duplicate code and improves project maintainability. 

Open the following file:

db/common.cds

Add the following code: 

namespace dip.common; 
using { } from '@sap/cds/common'; 

type Gender : String(20) enum 
{ 
  Male          = 'M'; 
  Female        = 'F'; 
  nonBinary     = 'N'; 
  noDisclosure  = 'D'; 
  selfDescribe  = 'S'; 
}; 

type AmountT : Decimal(15, 2) @( 
  Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'CURRENCY_CODE', 
  sap.units                     : 'CURRENCY_CODE' 
); 

aspect Amount { 
  CURRENCY_CODE : String(4); 
  GROSS_AMOUNT  : AmountT; 
  NET_AMOUNT    : AmountT; 
  TAX_AMOUNT    : AmountT; 
} 

type PhoneNumber : String(14) 
  @assert.format.pattern : '((?:\\+|00)[17](?: |\\-)?|(?:\\+|00)[1-9]\\d{0,2}(?: |\\-)?|(?:\\+|00)1\\-\\d{3}(?: |\\-)?)(0\\d|([0-9]{3})|[1-9]{0,3})(?:((?: |\\-)[0-9]{2}){4}|((?:[0-9]{2}){4})|((?: |\\-)[0-9]{3}(?: |\\-)[0-9]{4})|([0-9]{7}))'; 

type Email : String(255) 
  @assert.format.pattern : '^([a-zA-Z0-9_\\-\\.]+)@([a-zA-Z0-9_\\-\\.]+)\\.([a-zA-Z]{2,5})$';
 
Step 4: Run the CAPM Application 

After creating the entity, service layer, and common types file, the next step is to run the SAP CAPM application. 

Open the terminal in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) and execute the following command: 

cds watch

The cds watch command starts the CAPM server and automatically reloads the application whenever changes are made in CDS files or service files. 

Once the application starts successfully, you will see the server running on: 

http://localhost:4004 

You can now access the OData services from the browser. 

 

Step 5: Open OData Service

Open the following URL in browser: 

localhost:4004/CatalogService/ 
You will see the available entity sets including: 

EmployeeSet 

 
Step 6: Create. http File for API Testing 

In SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), create a new file named: 

test.http 

The .http file is used to test APIs directly inside BAS without external tools like Postman. 

Add the following requests inside test.http: 

### employeeSet top 5 
GET http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet?$top=5 

### INSERT Employee 
POST http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet 
Content-Type: application/json 
{ 
  "nameFirst": "Dip", 
  "nameMiddle": "Ramesh", 
  "nameLast": "Ragho", 
  "nameInitials": "DRR", 
  "sex": "F", 
  "language": "en", 
  "phoneNumber": "9876500001", 
  "email": "raj1@gmail.com", 
  "loginName": "rajkumar1", 
  "Currency_code": "INR", 
  "salaryAmount": 56000, 
  "accountNumber": "123456789001", 
  "bankId": "CBI001", 
  "bankName": "Central Bank", 
  "validFrom": "2024-01-01", 
  "validTo": "2030-12-31" 
} 

### UPDATE Employee 
PATCH http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet('111e8400-e29b-41d4-a716-446655440001') 
Content-Type: application/json   

{ 
  "salaryAmount": 56000 
} 

### DELETE Employee 
DELETE http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet('111e8400-e29b-41d4-a716-446655440001')

Using .http files, developers can: 

  • test CRUD operations  
  • verify API responses  
  • test restrictions from '@Capabilities'  
  • test read-only behavior from '@readonly'

directly inside BAS. 

Run the POST request from the .http file to insert employee data. 

 
Step 7: Apply CRUD Restrictions Using '@readonly' and '@Capabilities'

After successfully testing the APIs, the next step is to apply CRUD restrictions on the EmployeeSet entity using '@readonly' and '@Capabilities' annotations. 

These annotations help control which operations users are allowed to perform on the entity. 

Using '@readonly'

The '@readonly' annotation makes the entity completely read-only. 

Only the GET operation is allowed. 
POST, PATCH, and DELETE operations are automatically restricted. 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Update the code as shown below: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 
service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService') 

{  
    @readonly 
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee; 
}

Testing '@readonly'

Now run the same API requests from the .http file. 

You will notice: 

  • GET request works successfully  
  • POST request fails  
  • PATCH request fails  
  • DELETE request fails  

Expected error response: 

405 Method Not Allowed 

Expected Output 

Below you can see the screenshots of the API responses generated during testing. 

successful GET response  

 

 failed POST response

 

failed PATCH response

 

failed DELETE response 

 
 Using '@Capabilities'

The '@Capabilities' annotation provides more flexibility by allowing developers to control Insert, Update, and Delete operations individually. 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Update the code as shown below: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 

service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService') {  

   @Capabilities : { 
   Insertable  : true, 
   Updatable   : false, 
   Deletable   : false 
} 
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee; 
}

Allowed and Restricted Operations 

Operation 

Status 

GET 

Allowed 

POST 

Allowed 

PATCH 

Restricted 

DELETE 

Restricted 

Testing '@Capabilities' 

Run the API requests again using the .http file. 

You will notice: 

  • GET request works  
  • POST request works  
  • PATCH request fails  
  • DELETE request fails  

Expected restricted response: 

405 Method Not Allowed 

Expected Output 

Below you can see the screenshots of the API responses generated during testing. 

GET success response  

 

POST success response  

 

PATCH restricted response

 

DELETE restricted response 

 

Conclusion 

In this blog, we learned how to restrict CRUD operations in SAP CAPM using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotations. We also explored how to test APIs directly inside SAP Business Application Studio using .http files. 

These annotations help developers secure APIs, control user operations, and protect business data from unwanted modifications. 

Thank you for reading this blog. I hope this blog helps you understand CRUD restriction concepts in SAP CAPM more effectively.

 

Thanks and Regards, 

Deepa Ramesh Ragho

entry.title: Securing SAP UI5 Applications with a Custom Node.js Backend using JWT
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

When I pair SAP UI5 with a custom Node.js backend, there's no BTP or XSUAA to provide security. JWT (JSON Web Token) is the right tool for providing security. It is stateless, lightweight, and works naturally with both Express middleware and the UI5 fetch API.

This guide covers the complete end-to-end setup:

  • MySQL — user table with bcrypt-hashed passwords and roles
  • Node.js backend — JWT generation on login, protected routes via middleware
  • SAP UI5 frontend — token storage, role-based routing, authenticated API calls, session expiry handling
  • Local dev proxy—ui5.yaml config so the UI5 dev server forwards /api calls to Node.js

 

Why I Wrote This

A few months back, I was working on a project where we had to build a lightweight internal tool on top of an SAP landscape — a UI5 frontend talking to a custom Node.js backend. The moment security came up in the design discussion, I realized there wasn't one clear, end-to-end guide covering this specific combination: SAP UI5 + Node.js + JWT + Role-Based Access Control.

This post walks through the complete flow: setting up the database, generating the JWT on login, storing it in the UI5 frontend, protecting your Node.js APIs with middleware, proxying API calls during local development, and handling session expiry gracefully.

 

First, Let's Understand JWT—The Wristband at the Door

Before jumping into code, here's how I explain JWT to anyone who's new to it.

Picture a nightclub with a VIP section. The first time you arrive, the bouncer checks your ID (your email and password). Once verified, they hand you a wristband — a JWT. For the rest of the night, you don't show your ID again. You just flash the wristband. The staff trusts it because the club itself issued it and it's tamperproof.

That's exactly what happens here:

  1. User submits their credentials to the Login API
  2. The backend verifies them against the database
  3. If valid, the server signs a JWT containing the user's ID, email, and role
  4. The UI5 app stores that token and attaches it to every subsequent request
  5. Each protected API checks the token before responding

Since the token is cryptographically signed with a secret key only the server knows, no one can fake or modify it.

 

Architecture Overview

The setup is a decoupled architecture—UI5 and Node.js live as completely separate projects and talk only through HTTP API calls. Here's the folder structure I used:

project-root/
│
├── backend/                        ← Node.js (Express) server
│   ├── .env                        ← Secret key and DB credentials (NEVER commit this)
│   ├── server.js                   ← Entry point — boots Express and registers routes
│   ├── repo/
│   │   └── dbconnection.js         ← MySQL connection setup
│   ├── middleware/
│   │   └── authMiddleware.js       ← JWT verification guard
│   ├── controller/
│   │   ├── userController.js       ← Login logic with bcrypt
│   │   └── adminController.js      ← Protected data APIs
│   └── routes/
│       ├── userRoutes.js           ← Public routes (login)
│       └── adminRoutes.js          ← Protected routes (require JWT)
│
└── frontend/                       ← SAP UI5 application
    ├── ui5.yaml                    ← Dev server config including API proxy
    ├── webapp/
    │   ├── controller/
    │   │   ├── View1.controller.js          ← Login page logic
    │   │   ├── AdminDashboard.controller.js
    │   │   └── UserDashboard.controller.js
    │   ├── view/
    │   │   ├── App.view.xml                 ← Root shell (contains App id="app")
    │   │   ├── View1.view.xml               ← Login screen
    │   │   ├── AdminDashboard.view.xml
    │   │   └── UserDashboard.view.xml
    │   └── manifest.json                    ← Router config lives here

 

Step 1: Setting Up MySQL

We need a database to store user credentials and roles. I'm using MySQL here. If you already have it installed, jump straight to the schema part.

Otherwise, install MySQL Community Server from the official MySQL site, along with MySQL Workbench for a visual interface.

Once you're logged into Workbench, open a new SQL tab and run this:

-- Create and select the database
CREATE DATABASE my_ui5_app;
USE my_ui5_app;

-- User table with role-based access
CREATE TABLE `user` (
  `user_id`        INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `user_name`      VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  `email`          VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  `contact_number` VARCHAR(15) DEFAULT NULL,
  `password`       VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,        
  `role`           VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'User',  
  PRIMARY KEY (`user_id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `email_UNIQUE` (`email`)
);

INSERT INTO `user` (`user_name`, `email`, `password`, `role`)
VALUES ('System Admin', 'admin@company.com', '$2b$10$cUSzmudHOeDXCF6eToP3buHZULwxuuJ/J5WZYeFkStg7HBpy2uCcW', 'Admin');

INSERT INTO `user` (`user_name`, `email`, `password`, `role`)
VALUES ('Shubham', 'shubham@gmail.com', '$2b$10$xBn6cKLkEuIkSqoLSR3pw.r16EABwE7aHtIDU4KSq2czl3LW5PMpK', 'User');

 

 

Step 2: Backend — Installing Dependencies and Generating Password Hashes

Let's get the backend ready first by using commands.

mkdir backend
cd backend
npm init -y
npm install express mysql2 jsonwebtoken bcrypt dotenv

Create a .env file in the backend root:

DB_HOST=localhost
DB_USER=root
DB_PASSWORD=your_mysql_password
DB_NAME=my_ui5_app
SECRET_KEY=your_very_long_random_secret_key_here
PORT=3306

Generating Real Password Hashes
Before inserting test users into MySQL, generate proper bcrypt hashes. Create generateHash.js in your backend folder—this is a one-time utility script, not part of the running app:

const bcrypt = require('bcrypt');

async function generateHashes() {
    const adminHash = await bcrypt.hash('Admin@123', 10);
    const userHash  = await bcrypt.hash('User@123', 10);

    console.log('Admin hash:', adminHash);
    console.log('User hash: ', userHash);
}

generateHashes();

 

Database Connection —repo/dbconnection.js

const mysql = require('mysql2');
require('dotenv').config();

const connection = mysql.createConnection({
    host:     process.env.DB_HOST,
    user:     process.env.DB_USER,
    password: process.env.DB_PASSWORD,
    database: process.env.DB_NAME
});

connection.connect((err) => {
    if (err) {
        console.error('DB connection failed:', err.message);
        return;
    }
    console.log('Connected to MySQL');
});

module.exports = connection;

 

Entry Point - server.js 
This boots the whole backend—wires together Express, registers the routes, and starts listening. I prefix all routes with /api to cleanly separate API traffic from any static file serving. 

const express = require('express');
const dotenv  = require('dotenv');

dotenv.config();

const userRoutes  = require('./routes/userRoutes');
const adminRoutes = require('./routes/adminRoutes');

const app  = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

// Parse incoming JSON request bodies
app.use(express.json());

// Register routes
app.use('/api/user',  userRoutes);  
app.use('/api/admin', adminRoutes);  

app.get('/api/health', (req, res) => {
    res.status(200).json({ status: 'Server is up and running' });
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
    console.log(`Backend running at http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});

Start the server by giving commands to the terminal:
node server.js
npx nodemon server.js

 

Step 3: Backend — Login API with bcrypt and JWT

When the UI5 frontend submits credentials, the backend needs to:

  1. Find the user in the DB by email
  2. Compare the submitted password against the stored bcrypt hash using bcrypt.compare()
  3. If it matches, sign a JWT containing the user's ID, email, and role.

controller/userController.js:

const jwt    = require('jsonwebtoken');
const bcrypt = require('bcrypt');
const db     = require('../repo/dbconnection');

async function loginUser(req, res) {
    const { email, password } = req.body;

    if (!email || !password) {
        return res.status(400).json({ message: "Email and password are required." });
    }

    // Fetch user by email 
    const query = 'SELECT * FROM user WHERE email = ?';

    db.query(query, [email], async (err, results) => {
        if (err) {
            console.error('DB error:', err);
            return res.status(500).json({ message: "Internal server error." });
        }

        if (results.length === 0) {
            return res.status(401).json({ message: "Invalid email or password." });
        }

        const user = results[0];

        // bcrypt.compare() checks the submitted password against the stored hash
        const passwordMatch = await bcrypt.compare(password, user.password);
        if (!passwordMatch) {
            return res.status(401).json({ message: "Invalid email or password." });
        }

        // Build the JWT payload
        const tokenPayload = {
            user_id: user.user_id,
            email:   user.email,
            role:    user.role
        };

        // Sign the token using the secret key from .env — expires in 24 hours
        const token = jwt.sign(
            tokenPayload,
            process.env.SECRET_KEY,
            { expiresIn: '24h' }
        );

        return res.status(200).json({
            message: "Login successful",
            token:   token,
            user:    { role: user.role, name: user.user_name }
        });
    });
}

module.exports = { loginUser };

 

routes/userRoutes:

const express       = require('express');
const router        = express.Router();
const { loginUser } = require('../controller/userController');

router.post('/login', loginUser);

module.exports = router;

 

Step 4: Backend — The Middleware Guard 

This is the piece that protects every route requiring authentication. Every request to a secured endpoint passes through this function before reaching the controller.

middleware/authMiddleware.js:

const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken');

const verifyToken = (req, res, next) => {
    const authHeader = req.headers['authorization'];

    // Reject immediately if no Authorization header is present
    if (!authHeader || !authHeader.startsWith('Bearer ')) {
        return res.status(403).json({ message: "Access denied. No token provided." });
    }

    // Strip the "Bearer" prefix to get the raw token string
    const token = authHeader.split(' ')[1];

    jwt.verify(token, process.env.SECRET_KEY, (err, decoded) => {
        if (err) {
            // Token is expired or has been tampered with
            return res.status(401).json({ message: "Session expired or invalid token." });
        }

        req.user = decoded;
        next();
    });
};

module.exports = { verifyToken };

 

To protect a route, inject this middleware between the route path and the controller function:

routes/adminRoutes:

const express            = require('express');
const router             = express.Router();
const { verifyToken }    = require('../middleware/authMiddleware');
const { getItemDetails } = require('../controller/adminController');

// verifing Token 
router.get('/getItemDetails', verifyToken, getItemDetails);

module.exports = router;

 

controller/adminController.js:

const db = require('../repo/dbconnection');

function getItemDetails(req, res) {
    const query = 'SELECT * FROM user';

    db.query(query, (err, results) => {
        if (err) {
            console.error('DB error:', err);
            return res.status(500).json({ message: "Internal server error." });
        }

        return res.status(200).json(results);
    });
}

module.exports = { getItemDetails };

 

Step 5: Frontend—Configuring manifest.json

Open manifest. JSON. First, add sap.ui.layout to libraries inside manifest.json since I am using the SimpleForm control in the login view.

"libs": {
    "sap.m": {},
    "sap.ui.core": {},
    "sap.ui.layout": {}
}

Then replace the entire routing section with this:

"routing": {
    "config": {
        "routerClass": "sap.m.routing.Router",
        "controlAggregation": "pages",
        "controlId": "app",
        "transition": "slide",
        "type": "View",
        "viewType": "XML",
        "path": "ui5nodeapp.view",
        "async": true,
        "viewPath": "ui5nodeapp.view"
    },
    "routes": [
        {
            "name": "RouteLogin",
            "pattern": "",
            "target": ["TargetLogin"]
        },
        {
            "name": "RouteAdminDashboard",
            "pattern": "admin",
            "target": ["TargetAdminDashboard"]
        },
        {
            "name": "RouteUserDashboard",
            "pattern": "user",
            "target": ["TargetUserDashboard"]
        }
    ],
    "targets": {
        "TargetLogin": {
            "id": "View1",
            "name": "View1",
            "viewLevel": 1
        },
        "TargetAdminDashboard": {
            "id": "AdminDashboard",
            "name": "AdminDashboard",
            "viewLevel": 2
        },
        "TargetUserDashboard": {
            "id": "UserDashboard",
            "name": "UserDashboard",
            "viewLevel": 2
        }
    }
}

 

Step 6: Frontend — Proxying API Calls in Development

The UI5 dev server runs on port 8080 by default, while the Node.js backend runs on port 3000. So, I added the backend port to 3000 in the ui5.yaml file.

server:
  customMiddleware:
    - name: fiori-tools-proxy
      afterMiddleware: compression
      configuration:
        ignoreCertErrors: false
        backend:
          - path: /api
            url: http://localhost:3000   # Forward all /api calls to the Node.js backend
        ui5:
          path:
            - /resources
            - /test-resources
          url: https://ui5.sap.com
    - name: fiori-tools-appreload
      afterMiddleware: compression
      configuration:
        port: 35729
        path: webapp
        delay: 300
    - name: fiori-tools-preview
      afterMiddleware: fiori-tools-appreload
      configuration:
        flp:
          theme: sap_horizon

 

Step 7: Frontend — The Login View (XML)

view/View1.view.xml:

<mvc:View
    controllerName="ui5nodeapp.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:f="sap.ui.layout.form"
    displayBlock="true">

    <Page title="Login" showHeader="false">
        <content>

            <VBox
                justifyContent="Center"
                alignItems="Center"
                height="100%"
                class="sapUiMediumMarginTop">

                <Panel
                    width="380px"
                    class="sapUiResponsivePadding sapUiMediumPadding">

                    <VBox alignItems="Center" class="sapUiSmallMarginBottom">
                        <Title
                            text="Welcome Back"
                            level="H2"
                            class="sapUiSmallMarginBottom"/>
                        <Label text="Sign in to continue"/>
                    </VBox>

                    <f:SimpleForm
                        editable="true"
                        layout="ResponsiveGridLayout"
                        labelSpanL="12"
                        labelSpanM="12"
                        emptySpanL="0"
                        emptySpanM="0"
                        columnsL="1"
                        columnsM="1">

                        <f:content>

                            <Label text="Email Address" required="true"/>
                            <Input
                                id="loginUserId"
                                placeholder="Enter your email"
                                type="Email"
                                width="100%"
                                submit=".onLoggedIn"/>

                            <Label text="Password" required="true"/>
                            <Input
                                id="loginPassword"
                                placeholder="Enter your password"
                                type="Password"
                                width="100%"
                                submit=".onLoggedIn"/>

                        </f:content>
                    </f:SimpleForm>

                    <VBox alignItems="Center" class="sapUiSmallMarginTop">
                        <Button
                            text="Login"
                            type="Emphasized"
                            width="100%"
                            press=".onLoggedIn"/>
                    </VBox>

                </Panel>
            </VBox>

        </content>
    </Page>

</mvc:View>

 

Step 8: Frontend — The Login Controller

The login controller sends credentials to the backend, stores the token, and routes the user based on their role.

controller/View1.controller.js:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], function (Controller, MessageToast) {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("ui5nodeapp.controller.View1", {

        onInit: function () {
        },

        onLoggedIn: async function () {
            const sEmail    = this.byId("loginUserId").getValue().trim();
            const sPassword = this.byId("loginPassword").getValue();

            if (!sEmail || !sPassword) {
                MessageToast.show("Please enter both email and password.");
                return;
            }

            try {
                const response = await fetch("/api/user/login", {
                    method:  "POST",
                    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                    body:    JSON.stringify({ email: sEmail, password: sPassword })
                });

                const result = await response.json();

                if (!response.ok) {
                    throw new Error(result.message || "Login failed.");
                }

                // sessionStorage clears token automatically when the tab is closed
                sessionStorage.setItem("myAppToken", result.token);
                sessionStorage.setItem("myAppUser",  JSON.stringify(result.user));

                // Route to the correct dashboard based on role
                const oRouter = this.getOwnerComponent().getRouter();
                if (result.user.role === "Admin") {
                    oRouter.navTo("RouteAdminDashboard");
                } else {
                    oRouter.navTo("RouteUserDashboard");
                }

            } catch (error) {
                MessageToast.show(error.message || "Something went wrong. Please try again.");
            }
        }
    });
});

 

After logging in, we can check the token by clicking "inspect," then "application," then "session storage," and here we can see the generated token. 

 

Step 9: Frontend — Making Secure API Calls from the Dashboard

Any time the dashboard needs protected data, it reads the token from sessionStorage and attaches it to the Authorization header. It also handles session expiry cleanly.

controller/Dashboard.controller.js:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/ui/model/json/JSONModel",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], function (Controller, JSONModel, MessageToast) {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("ui5nodeapp.controller.Dashboard", {

        onInit: function () {
            this.loadSecureData();
        },

        loadSecureData: async function () {
            const token = sessionStorage.getItem("myAppToken");

            if (!token) {
                this._redirectToLogin("No active session. Please log in.");
                return;
            }

            try {
                const response = await fetch("/api/admin/getItemDetails", {
                    method:  "GET",
                    headers: {
                        "Accept":        "application/json",
                        "Authorization": "Bearer " + token
                    }
                });

                // 401 = token expired or tampered or missing token header
                if (response.status === 401 || response.status === 403) {
                    this._redirectToLogin("Your session has expired. Please log in again.");
                    return;
                }

                if (!response.ok) {
                    throw new Error("Failed to load data. Please try again.");
                }

                const data   = await response.json();
                const oModel = new JSONModel(data);
                this.getView().setModel(oModel, "secureData");

            } catch (error) {
                console.error("API error:", error);
                MessageToast.show(error.message);
            }
        },

        _redirectToLogin: function (message) {
            sessionStorage.removeItem("myAppToken");
            sessionStorage.removeItem("myAppUser");
            MessageToast.show(message);
            this.getOwnerComponent().getRouter().navTo("RouteLogin");
        },

        onLogout: function () {
            this._redirectToLogin("You have been logged out.");
        }
    });
});

 

A Few Things to Keep in Mind:

On sessionStorage vs localStorage: I chose sessionStorage because it clears automatically when the browser tab closes, reducing the exposure window on shared machines. Both are vulnerable to XSS attacks, so consider a content security policy header in production.

HTTPS is non-negotiable: JWTs in transit are meaningless without HTTPS. Local development over HTTP is fine, but before you deploy anywhere, put the app behind HTTPS.

Token expiry: The 24-hour expiry works for many internal tools. For anything more sensitive, tighten it to 1–2 hours and consider adding a refresh token mechanism.

Role checks on the backend too: The frontend role-based routing is purely a UX decision — it is not a security control. Always enforce role checks on the backend as well. 

 

Conclusion:

That's the complete picture—from MySQL setup and real bcrypt password hashing through JWT generation and middleware protection on the backend to the UI5 frontend handling token storage, role-based routing, and secure API calls.

entry.title: Deploying a UI5 Library to Application Frontend Service
entry.content:
Background

In a previous blog, I introduced an approach to simplify the use of a custom UI5 library on SAP BTP: bundling the library directly into the consuming application at build time. This eliminates the need for destination-related configuration in the consuming application, which is otherwise required when the library is deployed independently to HTML5 Application Repository.

However, this approach has a drawback: whenever the library is updated, any consuming application that needs to pick up the change must be rebuilt and redeployed individually.

Recently, I came across the blog UI Composition with Application Frontend Service by @VadimTomnikov, which introduced a more elegant solution. When using Application Frontend Service, a consuming application can reference a separately deployed library simply by adding a route in its xs-app.json. No destination configuration is needed, and the library version can even be specified per application.

This blog describes how to deploy a UI5 library to Application Frontend Service and consume it from another application.

Note: Application Frontend Service is not a replacement for HTML5 Application Repository — the two services currently target different use cases. In particular, native Work Zone integration is not yet supported; applications can be accessed via a direct URL or published as a Business Solution, but cannot be onboarded through the standard channel-based integration. If your use case requires tight Work Zone integration, HTML5 Application Repository remains the appropriate choice.

Prerequisites

  • Basic understanding of Application Frontend Service. If you are new to it, refer to Introducing Application Frontend Service.
  • Application Frontend Service is subscribed in your BTP subaccount. For setup instructions, refer to this blog post.
  • A UI5 library and a consuming application. This blog uses the same library and application created in the previous blog. Refer to Steps 1 and 2 of that blog for setup instructions.

Steps Overview

  1. Configure the Consuming Application
  2. Configure the Library for Deployment to Application Frontend Service
  3. Deploy and Verify

1. Configure the Consuming Application

1.1 Generate Deployment Configuration

Generate the deployment configuration for the consuming application using the SAP Fiori tools. When prompted, select Cloud Foundry as the target and Add Application to Application Frontend Service as the Router Module.

Figure: Deployment Configuration dialog — select Application Frontend Service as the Router Module.

1.2 xs-app.json

Add a route for the library before the other routes. The application property references the deployed library by its ID, optionally followed by @ and the version.

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/resources/com/myorg/reuselib/(.*)$",
      "target": "/$1",
      "application": "com.myorg.reuselib@1.0.0"
    },
    ...
  ]
}

2. Configure the Library for Deployment to Application Frontend Service

When a UI5 library is generated using the "Fiori: Open Reusable Library Generator", the generated package.json includes a deploy-config script that runs fiori add deploy-config. However, this command does not support generating Cloud Foundry / Application Frontend deployment configuration for library projects — only ABAP targets are available. Therefore, the following files must be created manually.

Files to Create or Update

TypeFileDescription
Createmta.yamlMTA module and resources for Application Frontend and XSUAA
Createui5-deploy.yamlUI5 build configuration with ui5-task-zipper
Createxs-app.jsonRouting configuration for Application Frontend
Createxs-security.jsonXSUAA configuration
Updatepackage.jsonAdd build and deploy scripts, and ui5-task-zipper to devDependencies

2.1 ui5-deploy.yaml

Create this file based on the consuming application's ui5-deploy.yaml. When adapting it for a library project, two adjustments are required.

Set afterTask to buildThemes in ui5-task-zipper

The generateCachebusterInfo task used in application builds does not run for libraries. The last task in a library build is buildThemes. Set afterTask to buildThemes as shown below.

- name: ui5-task-zipper
  afterTask: buildThemes
  configuration:
    archiveName: commyorgreuselib
    relativePaths: true
    additionalFiles:
      - xs-app.json

Add the framework section

Without this, the buildThemes task fails because the library's .less files import from sap.ui.core themes, which must be available as a build dependency. Copy the framework section from ui5.yaml as-is.

framework:
  name: SAPUI5
  version: 1.146.0
  libraries:
    - name: sap.ui.core
    - name: themelib_sap_fiori_3

2.2 xs-app.json

Create this file based on the consuming application's xs-app.json. Since a library does not have an index.html or a logout page, remove the ^/index.html$ and ^/logout-page.html$ routes. The final result should look like this:

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/resources/(.*)$",
      "target": "/resources/$1",
      "authenticationType": "none",
      "destination": "ui5"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/test-resources/(.*)$",
      "target": "/test-resources/$1",
      "authenticationType": "none",
      "destination": "ui5"
    },
    {
      "source": "^(.*)$",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "app-front",
      "authenticationType": "ias"
    }
  ]
}

2.3 xs-security.json

Create this file based on the consuming application's xs-security.json. Update xsappname to a value that identifies the library. In this example, the namespace and library module name are concatenated (commyorgreuselib).

{
  "xsappname": "commyorgreuselib",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "description": "Security profile of called application",
  "scopes": [],
  "role-templates": []
}

2.4 mta.yaml

Create this file based on the consuming application's mta.yaml. Replace all occurrences of the application's ID with the library's ID (commyorgreuselib). The html5 module build command remains unchanged as npm install && npm run build:cf.

2.5 package.json

Add the following scripts to build and deploy the library, along with the dependencies they require:

"scripts": {
+  "build:cf": "ui5 build --clean-dest --config ui5-deploy.yaml --dest dist",
+  "build:mta": "rimraf resources mta_archives && mbt build",
-  "deploy": "fiori verify",
+  "deploy": "fiori cfDeploy",
 },
 "devDependencies": {
+  "ui5-task-zipper": "^3.6.0",
+  "mbt": "^1.2.49",
+  "rimraf": "^6.1.3"
 }

Note that the initial deploy script generated by the library template is fiori verify, which only validates the project configuration and does not actually deploy. Replace it with fiori cfDeploy.

3. Deploy and Verify

Run the following commands in both the library and the consuming application projects. Deploy the library first, then the consuming application.

npm run build:mta
npm run deploy

After deploying, verify that both applications are registered in Application Frontend Service:

afctl list

Open the consuming application in the browser and confirm that the dialog from the library opens correctly when the button is pressed.

Figure: Consuming application running on Application Frontend Service, showing the dialog provided by the separately deployed library.

4. Simplifying Library Deployment Configuration with a Yeoman Generator

As described in the previous sections, setting up the deployment configuration for a UI5 library requires creating and updating several files manually. To simplify this process, I created a Yeoman generator (with the help of Claude Code) that automates these steps.

Installation

npm install -g yo generator-ui5-library-af-deploy

Usage

Run the generator in the root directory of the library project:

cd path/to/your-ui5-library
yo ui5-library-af-deploy

The generator reads ui5.yaml and the src/ directory structure to detect the library namespace automatically. If metadata.name in ui5.yaml does not match the detected namespace, the generator asks whether to update it — answering yes is recommended, as the full namespace format is used as a common identifier across the generated files.

After running the generator, install the dependencies and deploy:

npm install
npm run build:mta
npm run deploy

Limitations

  • Targets Application Frontend Service only (not HTML5 Application Repository)
  • Authentication type is fixed to ias
  • Always creates new service instances. If you want to reuse existing instances, adjust mta.yaml after running the generator.

Conclusion

Application Frontend Service simplifies the consumption of UI5 libraries significantly compared to HTML5 Application Repository. Consuming applications only need a single route in xs-app.json to reference a separately deployed library, with no destination configuration required. The library version can also be specified per application, giving each consuming application independent control over which version it uses.

The main remaining challenge is the library deployment configuration itself, as the standard tooling does not generate it for library projects. The Yeoman generator introduced in this blog fills that gap by automating the creation of the necessary files.

The sample code is available at: https://github.com/miyasuta/fiori-use-library-appfrontend

entry.title: How to Generate and Download a Dynamic PDF from SAPUI5 Form Data Using Node.js
entry.content:
Introduction

As someone who works with SAP development, I often come across situations where users need to download data from a form as a PDF with just one click. I wanted to find a clean and simple way to make this happen, and that's what led me to build this solution. In this blog, I'll show how I connected a SAPUI5 form to a Node.js backend to dynamically generate and download a PDF.

Before we dive in, let me quickly walk through what we need to get started.

Prerequisites 

Before starting, make sure you have the following ready:

  • Node.js installed on your machine

  • SAPUI5 project ready (local or SAP BAS)

  • Basic knowledge of JavaScript

  • VS Code or any code editor

  • npm packages: express, cors, pdfkit

Here is a quick overview of what we are building. The user fills in the following employee details in a SAPUI5 form:

  • Employee Name

  • Employee ID

  • Department

  • Location

  • Joining Date

On clicking "Generate and Download PDF", the data is sent to Node.js which dynamically builds the PDF with the entered values and sends it back for download.

Step 1 — Set Up Node.js Project 

Open your terminal and run the following commands:

mkdir dynamic-pdf-demo 
cd dynamic-pdf-demo 
npm init -y 
npm install express cors pdfkit 


 Step 2 — Create Node.js Dynamic PDF API 

Create a file called server.js and add the following code:

const express = require('express'); 
const cors = require('cors'); 
const PDFDocument = require('pdfkit'); 
 
const app = express(); 
app.use(cors()); 
app.use(express.json()); // Required to read JSON from SAPUI5 
 
// Dynamic PDF Generation API 
app.post('/api/generate/pdf', (req, res) => { 
 
  // Step 1: Read dynamic data sent from SAPUI5 form 
  const { name, empId, department, location, joiningDate } = req.body; 
 
  // Step 2: Validate incoming data 
  if (!name || !empId || !department || !location) { 
    return res.status(400).json({ error: "All fields are required!" }); 
  } 
 
  // Step 3: Set response headers 
  res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/pdf'); 
  res.setHeader( 
    'Content-Disposition', 
    `attachment; filename=${empId}_report.pdf` 
  ); 
 
  // Step 4: Create PDF document 
  const doc = new PDFDocument({ margin: 50 }); 
  doc.pipe(res); 
 
  // ── PDF Content ── 
 
  // Title 
  doc.fontSize(22).fillColor('#003366') 
     .text('Employee Report', { align: 'center' }); 
 
  doc.moveDown(0.5); 
 
  // Generated date 
  doc.fontSize(11).fillColor('#666666') 
     .text(`Generated on: ${new Date().toLocaleDateString()}`, 
           { align: 'center' }); 
 
  doc.moveDown(); 
 
  // Top divider 
  doc.moveTo(50, doc.y).lineTo(550, doc.y) 
     .lineWidth(1).strokeColor('#003366').stroke(); 
 
  doc.moveDown(); 
 
  // Section heading 
  doc.fontSize(14).fillColor('#003366') 
     .text('Employee Details', { underline: true }); 
 
  doc.moveDown(0.5); 
 
  // Dynamic values from SAPUI5 form 
  doc.fontSize(12).fillColor('#000000'); 
  doc.text(`Name           :  ${name}`);         doc.moveDown(0.3); 
  doc.text(`Employee ID    :  ${empId}`);         doc.moveDown(0.3); 
  doc.text(`Department     :  ${department}`);    doc.moveDown(0.3); 
  doc.text(`Location       :  ${location}`);      doc.moveDown(0.3); 
  doc.text(`Joining Date   :  ${joiningDate || 'N/A'}`); 
 
  doc.moveDown(1.5); 
 
  // Bottom divider 
  doc.moveTo(50, doc.y).lineTo(550, doc.y) 
     .lineWidth(0.5).strokeColor('#cccccc').stroke(); 
 
  doc.moveDown(); 
 
  // Footer 
  doc.fontSize(10).fillColor('#888888') 
     .text('This document is dynamically generated by Node.js + SAPUI5.', 
           { align: 'center' }); 
 
  // Step 5: Finalize PDF 
  doc.end(); 
}); 
 
app.listen(3000, () => { 
  console.log('Server running on http://localhost:3000'); 
});

Once the file is ready, run the server using the following command:

node server.js

You should see the message Server running on http://localhost:3000 in your terminal.

Before connecting it to SAPUI5, I tested the API using Postman to make sure the endpoint was working correctly. To test it, send a POST request to http://localhost:3000/api/generate/pdf with the following JSON body:

{  "name": "John Doe",  "empId": "EMP-1001",  "department": "Information Technology",  "location": "Mysuru, Karnataka",  "joiningDate": "01-01-2023" }

If everything is set up correctly, the server will respond with a downloadable PDF file.

Step 3 — Create SAPUI5 Form in View.xml 

Now let us build the SAPUI5 form. Create your View.xml file and add the following code:

<mvc:View controllerName="project1.controller.View1" 
  xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc" 
  xmlns="sap.m" 
  xmlns:f="sap.ui.layout.form" 
  xmlns:l="sap.ui.layout" 
  xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"> 

  <Page title="Employee PDF Report Generator" showHeader="true"> 
    <content> 
      <VBox 
        class="sapUiMediumMarginTop sapUiMediumMarginBeginEnd sapUiMediumMarginEnd" 
        width="100%"> 

        <Panel 
          headerText="Employee Information" 
          expandable="false" 
          width="600px" 
          class="sapUiMediumMarginBottom"> 
          <content> 
            <f:SimpleForm 
              editable="true" 
              layout="ResponsiveGridLayout" 
              labelSpanXL="4" 
              labelSpanL="4" 
              labelSpanM="4" 
              labelSpanS="12" 
              adjustLabelSpan="false" 
              emptySpanXL="0" 
              emptySpanL="0" 
              emptySpanM="0" 
              columnsXL="1" 
              columnsL="1" 
              columnsM="1" 
              class="sapUiSmallMarginTop"> 

              <f:content> 
                <Label text="Employee Name" required="true" /> 
                <Input 
                  id="inpName" 
                  placeholder="Enter employee name" 
                  valueLiveUpdate="true" /> 

                <Label text="Employee ID" required="true" /> 
                <Input 
                  id="inpEmpId" 
                  placeholder="e.g. EMP-1001" 
                  valueLiveUpdate="true" /> 

                <Label text="Department" required="true" /> 
                <Input 
                  id="inpDept" 
                  placeholder="e.g. Information Technology" 
                  valueLiveUpdate="true" /> 

                <Label text="Location" required="true" /> 
                <Input 
                  id="inpLocation" 
                  placeholder="e.g. Mysuru, Karnataka" 
                  valueLiveUpdate="true" /> 

                <Label text="Joining Date" /> 
                <DatePicker 
                  id="inpDate" 
                  placeholder="DD-MM-YYYY" 
                  valueFormat="dd-MM-yyyy" 
                  displayFormat="dd-MM-yyyy" /> 
              </f:content> 
            </f:SimpleForm> 
          </content> 
        </Panel> 

        <HBox 
          alignItems="Center" 
          justifyContent="Start" 
          class="sapUiSmallMarginBottom" 
          width="600px"> 
          <Button 
            id="btnGenerate" 
            text="Generate and Download PDF" 
            type="Emphasized" 
            icon="sap-icon://pdf-attachment" 
            press="onGeneratePDF" 
            class="sapUiSmallMarginEnd" /> 
          <Button 
            text="Clear Form" 
            type="Transparent" 
            icon="sap-icon://clear-all" 
            press="onClearForm" /> 
        </HBox> 

        <HBox 
          alignItems="Center" 
          width="600px" 
          class="sapUiSmallMarginBottom"> 
          <Text 
            id="statusText" 
            text="" 
            class="sapUiSmallMarginTop" /> 
        </HBox> 
      </VBox> 
    </content> 
  </Page> 
</mvc:View>

Step 4 — Write Controller Logic

Now let us write the controller logic that handles form validation, sends data to Node.js, and triggers the PDF download:

sap.ui.define([ 
  "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller", 
  "sap/m/MessageToast", 
  "sap/m/MessageBox" 
], function (Controller, MessageToast, MessageBox) { 
  "use strict"; 

  return Controller.extend("project1.controller.View1", { 

    onGeneratePDF: function () { 
      var oButton = this.byId("btnGenerate"); 
      var oStatus = this.byId("statusText"); 

      // Step 1: Read form values 
      var sName     = this.byId("inpName").getValue().trim(); 
      var sEmpId    = this.byId("inpEmpId").getValue().trim(); 
      var sDept     = this.byId("inpDept").getValue().trim(); 
      var sLocation = this.byId("inpLocation").getValue().trim(); 
      var sDate     = this.byId("inpDate").getValue(); 

      // Step 2: Validate inputs 
      var bValid = true; 

      if (!sName) { 
        this.byId("inpName").setValueState("Error"); 
        this.byId("inpName").setValueStateText("Name is required!"); 
        bValid = false; 
      } else { 
        this.byId("inpName").setValueState("Success"); 
      } 

      if (!sEmpId) { 
        this.byId("inpEmpId").setValueState("Error"); 
        this.byId("inpEmpId").setValueStateText("Employee ID is required!"); 
        bValid = false; 
      } else { 
        this.byId("inpEmpId").setValueState("Success"); 
      } 

      if (!sDept) { 
        this.byId("inpDept").setValueState("Error"); 
        this.byId("inpDept").setValueStateText("Department is required!"); 
        bValid = false; 
      } else { 
        this.byId("inpDept").setValueState("Success"); 
      } 

      if (!sLocation) { 
        this.byId("inpLocation").setValueState("Error"); 
        this.byId("inpLocation").setValueStateText("Location is required!"); 
        bValid = false; 
      } else { 
        this.byId("inpLocation").setValueState("Success"); 
      } 

      // Step 3: Stop if validation fails 
      if (!bValid) { 
        MessageToast.show("Please fill all required fields!"); 
        return; 
      } 

      // Step 4: Show loading state 
      oButton.setEnabled(false); 
      oButton.setText("Generating PDF..."); 
      oStatus.setText(""); 

      // Step 5: Send form data to Node.js via POST 
      fetch("http://localhost:3000/api/generate/pdf", { 
        method: "POST", 
        headers: { 
          "Content-Type": "application/json" 
        }, 
        body: JSON.stringify({ 
          name: sName, 
          empId: sEmpId, 
          department: sDept, 
          location: sLocation, 
          joiningDate: sDate 
        }) 
      }) 
      .then(function (response) { 
        // Step 6: Check response status 
        if (!response.ok) { 
          throw new Error("Failed to generate PDF"); 
        } 
        // Step 7: Convert to Blob 
        return response.blob(); 
      }) 
      .then(function (blob) { 
        // Step 8: Create download link 
        var link = document.createElement('a'); 
        link.href = URL.createObjectURL(blob); 
        link.download = sEmpId + '_report.pdf'; // Dynamic filename! 
        document.body.appendChild(link); 
        link.click(); 
        document.body.removeChild(link); 

        // Step 9: Show success 
        MessageToast.show("PDF generated and downloaded successfully!"); 
        oStatus.setText("PDF generated for " + sName + " successfully!"); 
        oButton.setEnabled(true); 
        oButton.setText("Generate and Download PDF"); 
      }) 
      .catch(function () { 
        MessageToast.show("Error generating PDF!"); 
        oStatus.setText("PDF generation failed. Please try again."); 
        oButton.setEnabled(true); 
        oButton.setText("Generate and Download PDF"); 
      }); 
    }, 

    onClearForm: function () { 
      this.byId("inpName").setValue("").setValueState("None"); 
      this.byId("inpEmpId").setValue("").setValueState("None"); 
      this.byId("inpDept").setValue("").setValueState("None"); 
      this.byId("inpLocation").setValue("").setValueState("None"); 
      this.byId("inpDate").setValue(""); 
      this.byId("statusText").setText(""); 
      sap.m.MessageToast.show("Form cleared!"); 
    } 
  }); 
});

Output: 

Here is how the final output looks once the form is filled and the Generate and Download PDF button is clicked. The PDF is automatically downloaded to your system with the employee details neatly formatted inside it.

Conclusion

In this blog, we explored how to build an end-to-end PDF generation flow by integrating a SAPUI5 form with a Node.js backend. We covered everything from setting up the Node.js server with Express and pdfkit, designing the SAPUI5 form in View.xml, writing the controller logic for validation and API communication, to finally triggering an automatic PDF download in the browser. This approach can be very useful in real-world SAP projects where users need to export form data as formatted documents instantly without any manual effort.

entry.title: CRUD Operations with Image Upload using SAPUI5, Node.js, Express, MySQL, and Multer
entry.content:
Hi, 
 
Introduction: 

Media such as images, videos, and icons play an important role in making applications visually appealing and interactive, which helps improve user engagement. 

In this blog, we will learn how to perform CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations with image upload functionality using: 

  • SAPUI5 for the frontend  
  • Node.js and Express.js for the backend  
  • MySQL as the database  
  • Multer for handling file uploads 

Backend Implementation using Node.js

Prerequisite - Install MySQL Workbench/MySQL Server and create a database. 

 
Step 1 : Create a backend folder and inside it create a file named index.js 
And run the following commands  

  • npm init –y 
  • npm i express cors mysql2 multer 

 

Command 

Purpose 

npm init -y 

Initializes a Node.js project with default configurations 

npm i express 

Installs Express.js framework for backend APIs 

npm i cors 

Enables Cross-Origin Resource Sharing between frontend and backend 

npm i mysql2 

Connects Node.js application with MySQL database 

npm i multer 

Handles file uploads 

 
Step 2 : Create the uploads folder 

Note: Make sure the uploads folder exists before starting the server. 
Otherwise, multer may throw an error while uploading files. 

 
Step 3 : Add the below code in index.js 

Note : Enter the MySQL password (the one you created during MySQL Workbench/MySQL server Installation)

const express = require("express")
const cors = require("cors")
const mysql = require("mysql2")
const multer = require("multer")

const app = express()

app.use(express.json())
app.use(cors())

// Database Connection
const db = mysql.createConnection({
    host: 'localhost',
    user: 'root',
    password: 'Root@1234',
    database: 'student_db'
})

db.connect((err) => {
    if (err) {
        console.log("Database Connection Failed")
        console.log(err.message)
    } else {
        console.log("Database Connected Successfully")
    }
})

// Create Table
db.query(`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users(
id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
name VARCHAR(50),
city VARCHAR(50),
image VARCHAR(255)
)`, (err) => {
    if (err) {
        console.log("Table Creation Failed")
    } else {
        console.log("Users Table Ready")
    }
})


// Multer Storage
const storage = multer.diskStorage({
    destination: (req, file, cb) => {
        cb(null, "uploads")
    },
    filename: (req, file, cb) => {
        cb(null, Date.now() + "-" + file.originalname)
    }
})

const upload = multer({
    storage: storage
})


// Insert User
app.post("/users", upload.single("image"), (req, res) => {

    let name = req.body.name
    let city = req.body.city
    let image = req.file.filename

    db.query(
        "INSERT INTO users(name,city,image) VALUES(?,?,?)",
        [name, city, image],
        (err) => {
            if (err) {
                console.log("Failed to Insert User")
                return res.send(err)
            }

            console.log(`User Inserted : ${name}`)
            res.send("Inserted")
        }
    )
})


// Get Users
app.get("/users", (req, res) => {

    db.query("SELECT * FROM users", (err, result) => {

        if (err) {
            console.log("Failed to Fetch Users")
            return res.send(err)
        }

        console.log("Users Data Fetched")
        res.send(result)
    })
})


// Update User
app.put("/users/:id", upload.single("image"), (req, res) => {

    let id = req.params.id
    let name = req.body.name
    let city = req.body.city

    if (req.file) {

        let image = req.file.filename

        db.query(
            "UPDATE users SET name=?,city=?,image=? WHERE id=?",
            [name, city, image, id],
            (err) => {

                if (err) {
                    console.log(`Failed to Update User ID : ${id}`)
                    return res.send(err)
                }

                console.log(`User Updated With Image : ID ${id}`)
                res.send("Updated")
            }
        )

    } else {

        db.query(
            "UPDATE users SET name=?,city=? WHERE id=?",
            [name, city, id],
            (err) => {

                if (err) {
                    console.log(`Failed to Update User ID : ${id}`)
                    return res.send(err)
                }

                console.log(`User Updated : ID ${id}`)
                res.send("Updated")
            }
        )
    }
})


// Delete User
app.delete("/users/:id", (req, res) => {

    let id = req.params.id

    db.query(
        "DELETE FROM users WHERE id=?",
        [id],
        (err) => {

            if (err) {
                console.log(`Failed to Delete User ID : ${id}`)
                return res.send(err)
            }

            console.log(`User Deleted : ID ${id}`)
            res.send("Deleted")
        }
    )
})


// Static Folder
app.use("/uploads", express.static("uploads"))


// Server Start
app.listen(3000, () => {
    console.log("Server Running Successfully")
    console.log("URL : http://localhost:3000")
})

Code explanation - Initially, all the required packages are imported. Then a connection to MySQL is established, and an uploads folder is used to store uploaded images. 

Here, the database name is student_db. After verifying the database connection, the application creates a table named users table inside the database (if table does not exists). 

Multer stores uploaded files on the server disk. All uploaded images are saved inside the uploads folder. To avoid duplicate file names, the uploaded image name is prefixed with the current timestamp using Date.now(). 

 
const upload = multer({ 
    storage: storage 
}) 

This connects multer with storage. 

upload.single("image") 

This middleware is used to upload a single file. 

  • "image" represents the field name  
  • The frontend or Postman form-data key must also be named image 

Then create API calls for Create, Read, Update and Delete 

app.use("/uploads", express.static("uploads")) 

This line exposes the uploads folder as a static directory so that uploaded images can be accessed directly through a URL. 

Example: 

uploads/17434img.png 

Access via: 

http://localhost:port_number/uploads/img_name.jpg 

The listen() method starts the server. 
The first parameter represents the port number on which the application runs. 

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 

Frontend Implementation using SAPUI5

Create the SAPUI5 application using basic template. 

In the view, create a form that accepts: 

  • Name  
  • City  
  • Image upload 

To upload images in SAPUI5, we use: 

  • Library: sap.ui.unified  
  • Control: FileUploader 

Three buttons are created for performing below operations: 

  • Create  
  • Update  
  • Delete  

 
After submitting the form, the user data is displayed in a list along with: 

  • User image  
  • Name  
  • City 

This is View1.view.xml file

<mvc:View controllerName="imgupdismulter.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns:u="sap.ui.unified"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <VBox class="sapUiSmallMargin">
            <HBox justifyContent="End" class="sapUiSmallMarginBottom">
                <Button text="Update" press="onUpdate" class="sapUiTinyMarginEnd"/>
                <Button text="Delete" press="onDelete" />
            </HBox>
            <!-- Form -->
            <Input id="name" placeholder="Enter Name" required="true" />
            <Input id="city" placeholder="Enter City" required="true" />
            <u:FileUploader 
                id="fileUploader" 
                name="image" 
                change="onFileSelect" required="true"/>
            <Button text="Create" press="onCreate" class="sapUiTinyMarginEnd"/>
            <List id="userList" />
        </VBox>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>


View Explanation 

  • Input controls are used for entering user details.  
  • FileUploader is used to select images.  
  • Button controls trigger CRUD operations.  
  • List displays all user records.  
  • Image control shows image preview before upload. 


This is View1.controller.js file

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/m/StandardListItem"
], (Controller, MessageToast, StandardListItem) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("imgupdismulter.controller.View1", {

        onInit() {
            this.loadData();
        },

        loadData: function () {
            debugger
            fetch("http://localhost:3000/users")
                .then(res => res.json())
                .then(data => {
                    let list = this.byId("userList");
                    list.removeAllItems();

                    data.forEach(user => {
                        list.addItem(
                            new StandardListItem({
                                title: user.name,
                                description: user.city,
                                icon: "http://localhost:3000/uploads/" + user.image,
                                type: "Active",
                                press: () => {
                                    debugger
                                    this.selectedId = user.id;
                                    this.byId("name").setValue(user.name);
                                    this.byId("city").setValue(user.city);
                                }
                            })
                        );
                    });
                });
        },

        onFileSelect: function (e) {
            debugger
            let file = e.getParameter("files")[0];
            this.selectedFile = file;
            this.selectedFileName = file.name;

            let reader = new FileReader();
            reader.onload = (evt) => {
                this.byId("preview").setSrc(evt.target.result);
            };
            reader.readAsDataURL(file);
        },

        onCreate: function () {

            let oName = this.byId("name");
            let oCity = this.byId("city");
            let oFile = this.byId("fileUploader");

            let name = oName.getValue().trim();
            let city = oCity.getValue().trim();

            // Regex
            let nameRegex = /^[A-Za-z ]{3,30}$/;
            let cityRegex = /^[A-Za-z ]{2,30}$/;

            // Reset Value States
            oName.setValueState("None");
            oCity.setValueState("None");
            oFile.setValueState("None");

            let isValid = true;

            // Name Validation
            if (!name) {

                oName.setValueState("Error");
                oName.setValueStateText("Name is required");

                isValid = false;

            } else if (!nameRegex.test(name)) {

                oName.setValueState("Error");
                oName.setValueStateText(
                    "Name must contain only letters and spaces (3-30 characters)"
                );

                isValid = false;
            }

            // City Validation
            if (!city) {

                oCity.setValueState("Error");
                oCity.setValueStateText("City is required");

                isValid = false;

            } else if (!cityRegex.test(city)) {

                oCity.setValueState("Error");
                oCity.setValueStateText(
                    "City must contain only letters and spaces (2-30 characters)"
                );

                isValid = false;
            }

            // File Validation
            if (!this.selectedFile) {

                oFile.setValueState("Error");
                oFile.setValueStateText("Image is required");

                isValid = false;
            }

            // Stop execution if validation fails
            if (!isValid) {

                MessageToast.show("Please enter valid data");

                return;
            }

            let formData = new FormData();

            formData.append("name", name);
            formData.append("city", city);
            formData.append("image", this.selectedFile);

            fetch("http://localhost:3000/users", {
                method: "POST",
                body: formData
            }).then(() => {

                MessageToast.show("Created Successfully");

                this.loadData();

                // Clear fields
                oName.setValue("");
                oCity.setValue("");
                oFile.clear();

                this.byId("preview").setSrc("");

                this.selectedFile = null;
                this.selectedFileName = null;
            });
        },

        onUpdate: function () {
            let name = this.byId("name").getValue();
            let city = this.byId("city").getValue();

            let formData = new FormData();
            formData.append("name", name);
            formData.append("city", city);

            if (this.selectedFile)
                formData.append("image", this.selectedFile);

            fetch("http://localhost:3000/users/" + this.selectedId, {
                method: "PUT",
                body: formData
            }).then(() => {
                MessageToast.show("Updated");
                this.loadData();
            });
        },

        onDelete: function () {
            fetch("http://localhost:3000/users/" + this.selectedId, {
                method: "DELETE"
            }).then(() => {
                MessageToast.show("Deleted");
                this.loadData();
            });
        }
    });
});

Controller Logic Explanation :

  • onInit() loads existing user data when the application starts.  
  • loadData() fetches all records from the backend and displays them in the SAPUI5 list.  
  • onFileSelect() reads the selected image and displays a preview.  
  • onCreate() sends form data to the backend using a POST request.  
  • onUpdate() updates existing user details using a PUT request.  
  • onDelete() removes a user record using a DELETE request. 

Note - First run the backend project using cli in the terminal ---- npm start or node index.js
make sure that "Database Connected Successfully" and running in the mentioned port ,
then run frontend project using cli in the terminal ---- npm run start 

Output :
After creation 

After updating

After Deleting

Conclusion : In this blog, we implemented CRUD operations with image handling using SAPUI5, Node.js, Express.js, MySQL, and Multer. 

The application allows users to: 

  • Upload images  
  • Store user information in MySQL  
  • Retrieve and display images dynamically  
  • Update existing records  
  • Delete records efficiently  

Multer is used for handling file uploads, while Express APIs manage backend operations. SAPUI5 provides a simple and interactive frontend for managing user data and displaying uploaded images.

entry.title: Reusable validation framework approach in UI5
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, in this blog I'll walk you through one of the approach to building a reusable validation framework in SAPUI5.

Form validation plays a critical role in any SAPUI5 application. The problem with the usual approach is that we end up writing multiple functions across different controllers to handle the same logic — repetitive, hard to maintain, and honestly just tedious. The idea here is simple: write the validation once, reuse it everywhere.

When I started working, I was writing multiple Functions in Multiple Controllers for Validation. After this I thought of process this and make it reusable. To achieve this I have moved my validation to separate file and I'm calling this function in a controller where I wanted.

To demonstrate this, we'll build a basic employee signup form and validate three required fields — Name, Email, and Phone Number. Nothing fancy, just a real-world scenario where data quality matters from the start.

SAPUI5 has built-in support for this through value states and event handling, both of which we'll take advantage of throughout. Below is the folder structure of the application we'll be building

 

In this blog I have Built One Application in this Below image will show you folder structure of my application

 
I have a signup form in a view that looks like the image shown below.
 
Here's the code written in View1.view.xml:
        <form:SimpleForm id="_IDGenSimpleForm1" editable="true">
            <Label id="_IDGenLabel" text="Name"></Label>
            <Input id="inpName" required="true" value="{Name}" width="30%"/>
            <Label id="_IDGenLabel1" text="Email"></Label>
            <Input id="inpEmail" required="true" value="{Email}" width="30%"/>
            <Label id="_IDGenLabel2" text="Phone"></Label>
            <Input id="inpPhone" required="true" value="{Phone}" width="30%"/>
            <Label id="_IDGenLabel3" ></Label>
            <Button id="_IDGenButton" text="Save" press="onSave" width="10%" ></Button>
        </form:SimpleForm>
Next, I created a ValidationHelper.js file inside the utils folder. This is where all the validation logic lives. Here's what's inside:
sap.ui.define([], function () {
    "use strict";
    return {
        validateRequiredFields: function (oView) {
            var bValid = true;
            var aControls = oView.findAggregatedObjects(true, function (oControl) {
                return oControl.getRequired && oControl.getRequired();
            });
            aControls.forEach(function (oControl) {
                var sValue = "";
                if (oControl.getValue) {
                    sValue = oControl.getValue();
                }
                if (!sValue) {
                    oControl.setValueState("Error");
                    oControl.setValueStateText("This field is mandatory");
                    bValid = false;
                } else {
                    oControl.setValueState("None");
                }
            });
            return bValid;
        }
    };
});

The function scans the view for any controls marked as required, checks whether they have a value, and sets the value state accordingly — Error if empty, None if filled. It returns a boolean so the controller knows whether to proceed.

Finally, in View1.controller.js, I imported ValidationHelper.js and called the function on save:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "project1/utils/ValidationHelper"
], function (Controller, MessageToast, ValidationHelper) {
    return Controller.extend("project1.controller.View1", {
        onSave: function () {
            if (!ValidationHelper.validateRequiredFields(this.getView())) {
                return;
            }
            MessageToast.show("Validation Passed");
        }
    });
});

Clean and straightforward — the controller doesn't need to know anything about how validation works. It just calls the helper and reacts to the result. That's the whole point of this approach. Any controller in your application can now reuse the same validation logic with a single line.

As I told you earlier, I have Created ValidatationHelper.js in Utils folder Where I have written Validation Logics and I'm calling it in View1. controller.js 

In this blog you have learnt How to reuse the validation codes and also you learnt How to call functions which are present in different files.


Thank you

entry.title: Handling Dynamic Columns in SAPUI5 Tables Without Duplicate ID Issues
entry.content:
Introduction

Dynamic table generation is a common requirement in SAPUI5 applications. One scenario that I recently worked on involved displaying a few business attributes as fixed columns while generating additional columns dynamically based on data returned from the backend.

For example, in a supplier comparison application, some columns remain fixed:

  • Material
  • Description
  • Plant

The remaining columns are generated dynamically depending on the number of suppliers received from the backend.

While implementing this requirement, I came across an issue that many SAPUI5 developers may have already faced — duplicate ID errors during table refreshes, especially when using createId() while recreating dynamic columns.

In this blog, I would like to share my observations, the root cause of the issue, and an approach that helped me handle dynamic columns in a cleaner and more maintainable way.

Picture 1: Understanding of Duplicate ID Error

 

Typical Implementation

The table structure consists of two parts as follows:

Fixed Columns (XML View)

<!-- Fixed Columns (XML View) -->
<Table id="supplierTable">
    <columns>
        <Column>
            <Text text="Material"/>
        </Column>
        <Column>
            <Text text="Description"/>
        </Column>
    </columns>
</Table>

 Dynamic Columns (Controller - Js File)

// Dynamic Columns (Controller)
aSuppliers.forEach(function(oSupplier) {
    oTable.addColumn(
        new sap.m.Column(
            this.createId("supplierColumn"),
            {
                header: new sap.m.Text({
                    text: oSupplier.Name
                })
            }
        )
    );
}.bind(this));

During the initial load, everything works as expected.

However, when the table is refreshed due to subsequent operations—such as an item-level POST operation, rebinding after user selections, or reloading data based on changed business conditions—the application may throw an error indicating that a control with the same ID already exists.

At first glance, it is easy to assume that createId() is the reason behind the issue.

 

Understanding the Root Cause

Initially, I thought createId() was generating duplicate IDs. However, after debugging the application flow, I realized that createId() was actually doing exactly what it was supposed to do.

this.createId("supplierColumn");

This consistently generates a view-scoped ID such as: MyView--supplierColumn

The real problem is not ID generation.

The actual issue occurs because the previously generated dynamic column is still present in the control hierarchy when the application attempts to create the same column again.

In other words, the issue is related to: Control Lifecycle Management rather than ID Generation

This understanding completely changed the way I approached the solution.

 

Can an isDynamic Flag Help?

While exploring different implementations, I noticed that many projects introduce a property such as:

isDynamic : true

The purpose of this flag is to identify tables whose structure is generated dynamically at runtime.

Before rebuilding the table structure, logic similar to the following can be executed:

if (this.isDynamic) {
    // cleanup logic
}

From my perspective, the important thing to understand is that the flag itself does not solve duplicate ID issues.

What actually resolves the issue is the cleanup operation that runs before recreating the controls.

The isDynamic flag simply acts as an indicator that the table structure needs to be refreshed.

Therefore, its value lies in controlling the rebuild process rather than preventing duplicate IDs directly.

 

Why destroyColumns() Is Not Always the Best Choice

One straightforward solution is:

oTable.destroyColumns();

This certainly removes the duplicate ID issue.

However, there is a drawback.

If the table contains both fixed columns defined in XML and dynamic columns created in JavaScript, destroyColumns() removes everything.

As a result, even the static columns must be recreated, which may not be necessary.

For smaller applications this may be acceptable, but for larger enterprise applications it introduces additional processing and maintenance effort.

 

Recommended Approach (How I sorted this issue)

The approach that made the most sense to me was to manage only the dynamically generated columns.

 

Picture 2: Recommended Approach

Step 1: Store References to Dynamic Columns

this._aDynamicColumns = [];

Step 2: Create Dynamic Columns

var oColumn = new sap.m.Column({
    header: new sap.m.Text({
        text: oSupplier.Name
    })
});
oTable.addColumn(oColumn);
this._aDynamicColumns.push(oColumn);

Step 3: Cleanup Before Refresh

this._aDynamicColumns.forEach(function(oColumn) {
    oTable.removeColumn(oColumn);
    oColumn.destroy();
});
this._aDynamicColumns = [];

Step 4: Rebuild Dynamic Columns

Once the cleanup is completed, the latest supplier columns can be recreated based on the newest backend response.

This ensures that the fixed XML-defined columns remain untouched while only the dynamic portion of the table is regenerated.

 

Benefits of This Approach

I found several advantages with this implementation:

  • Prevents duplicate ID errors
  • Preserves XML-defined static columns
  • Avoids unnecessary recreation of controls
  • Improves maintainability
  • Keeps a clear separation between static and dynamic content
  • Makes future enhancements and debugging easier

 Picture 3: Benefits of current approach

 

Conclusion

While working on dynamic table generation in SAPUI5, I initially assumed that createId() was responsible for duplicate ID errors. After investigating the issue, I realized that the real challenge was managing the lifecycle of dynamically created controls.

Using an isDynamic flag can be helpful for identifying when a table structure needs to be rebuilt, but it should not be viewed as the actual solution. The key is ensuring that previously generated dynamic controls are properly removed or destroyed before creating new ones.

For applications that combine fixed XML columns with runtime-generated columns, tracking and cleaning up only the dynamic columns is, in my opinion, one of the most practical and maintainable approaches.

If you have any alternative effective solutions, please feel free to share your thoughts and so everyone can learn from each other.

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog! If you found this helpful, i would love to hear your thoughts, feedback or questions in the comments. Let's keep learning and growing together!

I’m still new to blogging, so if you notice anything that could be improved or corrected, please don’t hesitate to let me know!!

entry.title: Message Popover in UI5
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Hope you are doing well.

In this blog, I am going to explain how to create and use a Message Popover in a SAPUI5 application.
